stem: What is the definitive surgical treatment for UPJ obstruction?

choices.feedback: Correct! Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty: excise the adynamic UPJ segment → spatulate the ureter → reanastomose to the renal pelvis (may reduce redundant pelvis). Success rate: > 95%. Can be performed: open, laparoscopic, or robotic-assisted. Also addresses crossing vessels (ureter transposed anterior to the vessel). For infants: performed when function is declining or < 40%. Many mild cases resolve spontaneously and are monitored with serial US/renograms.
Nephrectomy is reserved only for non-functioning kidneys (< 10% function) where preservation is not worthwhile. Pyeloplasty is the reconstructive option.
Stents can temporize but do not address the adynamic segment. Definitive repair requires pyeloplasty.
Endopyelotomy (incision) or balloon dilation has lower success rates (~60-80%) compared to pyeloplasty (>95%) and is generally reserved for select adult cases, not first-line.

explanation: Correct: Dismembered pyeloplasty (Anderson-Hynes).
verifiedExplanation: Dismembered pyeloplasty (Anderson-Hynes) is the definitive surgical treatment for UPJ obstruction, with success rates exceeding 95%. The procedure excises the narrowed UPJ segment and reanastomoses the renal pelvis to the ureter in a spatulated, tension-free fashion. It can be performed open, laparoscopically, or robotically. Indications include declining split function (<40%), symptomatic obstruction (pain, infections), or progressive hydronephrosis. Many mild cases of prenatal UPJ obstruction resolve spontaneously and are managed with surveillance.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: dismembered pyeloplasty with success rate exceeding 95%

stem: A 2-week-old with prenatal hydronephrosis has a postnatal ultrasound showing SFU Grade 3 hydronephrosis. No ureteral dilation. What is the next step?

choices.feedback: Correct! Management pathway for prenatal hydronephrosis: postnatal US at 1-2 weeks → if Grade 3-4 hydronephrosis: MAG3 at 4-6 weeks (kidneys need time to mature for accurate GFR measurement). Start antibiotic prophylaxis pending evaluation. MAG3 results guide management: good function (>40%) + adequate drainage → observe with serial imaging; decreased function (<40%) or poor drainage (T½ >20 min) → pyeloplasty. Grade 1-2: usually observe with serial US alone.

VCUG is indicated if there is concern for VUR (ureteral dilation, bilateral hydronephrosis, thickened bladder). Isolated hydronephrosis without ureteral dilation suggests UPJ, and MAG3 is the appropriate next study.
Not all hydronephrosis requires surgery. Function must be assessed first to determine if obstruction is significant.
CT is avoided in infants due to radiation. US and MAG3 provide all necessary information for UPJ evaluation.

explanation: Best answer: MAG3 diuretic renogram at 4-6 weeks to assess drainage and function.
verifiedExplanation: For a 2-week-old with prenatal hydronephrosis and SFU Grade 3 on postnatal ultrasound, the next step is a MAG3 diuretic renogram at approximately 4-6 weeks of age. The postnatal US confirms persistent hydronephrosis, and the MAG3 provides critical information about differential renal function and drainage pattern to guide management. VCUG may also be obtained to rule out vesicoureteral reflux. Antibiotic prophylaxis is typically started while awaiting further evaluation.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: MAG3 renogram for evaluating renal function and drainage in postnatal hydronephrosis

stem: What is the most common cause of UPJ obstruction?

choices.feedback: Ureteral duplication causes its own distinct pathology (Weigert-Meyer rule) but is not the cause of isolated UPJ obstruction.
Retroperitoneal fibrosis can cause ureteral obstruction in adults but is not the cause of congenital UPJ obstruction.

choices.text: Intrinsic adynamic segment with absent peristalsis at the junction
choices.feedback: Correct! Most common cause: intrinsic — an adynamic aperistaltic segment at the UPJ, likely due to abnormal smooth muscle arrangement or deficient neural innervation → urine cannot be propelled from pelvis to ureter → functional obstruction. Second most common: extrinsic compression by a crossing lower pole renal vessel (more common in older children/adults presenting with intermittent symptoms). Both are treated with dismembered pyeloplasty.

choices.text: Renal stones lodged at the UPJ
choices.feedback: Stones can cause secondary UPJ obstruction but are not the most common cause of congenital UPJ obstruction.

verifiedExplanation: The most common cause of UPJ obstruction is an intrinsic adynamic segment with absent or disordered peristalsis at the junction. Histologically, this segment shows abnormal smooth muscle arrangement with excess collagen deposition, preventing normal propagation of peristaltic waves from the renal pelvis to the ureter. Less common causes include a crossing lower pole renal vessel (which externally compresses the UPJ), kinking from a high-insertion ureter, and rarely polyps or strictures.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: intrinsic adynamic segment with abnormal smooth muscle and collagen at the UPJ

stem: A 55-year-old presents with the classic triad of flank pain, hematuria, and palpable flank mass. CT shows a 6 cm enhancing renal mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Oncocytoma is benign and typically an incidental finding. The classic triad with an enhancing mass strongly suggests malignancy.
AML contains fat (visible on CT as negative Hounsfield units). An enhancing solid mass without fat is more likely RCC.

choices.text: Transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis
choices.feedback: TCC typically presents as a filling defect in the renal pelvis on CT urogram, not as a solid enhancing cortical mass.

Correct! Classic triad (flank pain + hematuria + flank mass) occurs in only ~10% of RCC but is pathognomonic when present. Clear cell RCC: most common subtype (~70-80%), arises from proximal tubular epithelium, associated with VHL gene (chromosome 3p deletion). Highly vascular (enhances brightly on CT). Paraneoplastic syndromes: erythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without mets). Treatment: partial (T1) or radical nephrectomy. Immunotherapy/targeted therapy for metastatic.

explanation:
Correct Answer: Renal cell carcinoma

Renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type).

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Renal oncocytoma: Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.
- Angiomyolipoma: Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.
- Transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis: Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.

stem: Which paraneoplastic syndrome is most commonly associated with renal cell carcinoma?

choices.feedback: Ectopic ACTH causing Cushing syndrome is more commonly associated with small cell lung cancer and carcinoid tumors.
Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is associated with small cell lung cancer, not RCC.
SIADH is more commonly associated with small cell lung cancer.

Correct! RCC produces PTHrP → humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy. Other paraneoplastic associations: erythrocytosis (ectopic EPO production), hypertension (ectopic renin), Stauffer syndrome (non-metastatic hepatic dysfunction — elevated ALP, prolonged PT, hepatosplenomegaly — resolves with nephrectomy), and secondary amyloidosis. RCC is called the 'internist's tumor' because of its diverse paraneoplastic presentations.

explanation: Correct: Hypercalcemia (from PTHrP secretion).
verifiedExplanation: Hypercalcemia is the most common paraneoplastic syndrome associated with RCC, caused by secretion of PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related peptide). RCC is known as the 'internist's tumor' due to its diverse paraneoplastic manifestations, which also include erythrocytosis (from erythropoietin production), hypertension (from renin secretion), and Stauffer syndrome (nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction). Other paraneoplastic findings include fever, cachexia, and secondary amyloidosis.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: paraneoplastic syndromes including hypercalcemia from PTHrP secretion

stem: What genetic syndrome is associated with bilateral/multifocal clear cell RCC?

choices.feedback: Correct! VHL (autosomal dominant, chromosome 3p25): loss of VHL → constitutive HIF activation → upregulation of VEGF, PDGF → angiogenesis. Tumors: bilateral/multifocal clear cell RCC (most common cause of death in VHL), cerebellar/spinal hemangioblastomas, retinal angiomas, pheochromocytoma, pancreatic cysts/neuroendocrine tumors. Sporadic RCC also commonly has somatic VHL mutations. Screening: annual imaging starting in teens.
Li-Fraumeni predisposes to sarcomas, breast cancer, brain tumors, and adrenocortical carcinoma — not typically RCC.
TSC is associated with renal angiomyolipomas (AML) and rarely RCC. AMLs are the characteristic renal lesion in TSC.
BRCA2 is associated with breast, ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancers — not typically RCC.

explanation:
Correct Answer: Von Hippel-Lindau disease

Von Hippel-Lindau disease (VHL gene mutation).

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53): Does not align with the clinical presentation and pathophysiology described in this case.

stem: A 4 cm solid enhancing renal mass is found incidentally on CT in a 60-year-old. What is the recommended treatment?

choices.feedback: Radical nephrectomy is overtreatment for a 4 cm mass when partial nephrectomy is feasible. Adrenalectomy is only added if imaging shows adrenal involvement.
While active surveillance is an option for very small (< 2 cm) or fragile patients, a 4 cm solid enhancing mass warrants intervention in a 60-year-old.

Correct! For T1a tumors (≤ 4 cm): partial nephrectomy is the standard of care when technically feasible. Preserves renal function (reduces CKD risk) with equivalent oncologic outcomes to radical nephrectomy. Can be performed: open, laparoscopic, or robotic-assisted. T1b (4-7 cm): partial or radical depending on tumor location. T2+ or locally advanced: radical nephrectomy. Biopsy is not required before surgery for solid enhancing renal masses (>90% are malignant).
RCC is resistant to traditional chemotherapy. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is not standard for localized disease. Upfront surgery is the treatment.

verifiedExplanation: For a small (≤4 cm, T1a) solid enhancing renal mass, partial nephrectomy (nephron-sparing surgery) is the preferred approach. It provides equivalent oncologic outcomes to radical nephrectomy while preserving nephron mass and reducing the long-term risk of chronic kidney disease. Radical nephrectomy is generally reserved for larger tumors (>7 cm) or tumors with unfavorable characteristics. Over 50% of RCC cases are now found incidentally on imaging, making nephron-sparing approaches increasingly important.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: Partial nephrectomy is preferred when possible as it preserves kidney function

stem: RCC is classically resistant to which treatment modality?

choices.feedback: RCC is actually one of the most immunotherapy-responsive tumors. Checkpoint inhibitors have dramatically improved metastatic RCC outcomes.
Surgery (nephrectomy) is the mainstay curative treatment for localized RCC.
While RCC was historically called radioresistant, stereotactic body radiation (SBRT) has shown activity for primary tumors and oligometastases. Chemoresistance is the more classic teaching point.

Correct! RCC is notoriously chemoresistant (response rates < 5% to most cytotoxic agents). Mechanisms: high P-glycoprotein expression (MDR1 efflux pump) and anti-apoptotic pathways. Modern treatment for metastatic RCC: immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and VEGF-targeted TKIs (sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib). IL-2 (high-dose) was the first immunotherapy to show durable responses. Cytoreductive nephrectomy may still benefit select patients with metastatic disease.

verifiedExplanation: RCC is classically resistant to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy, which is a distinguishing feature of this malignancy. This resistance is attributed to expression of multi-drug resistance gene (MDR-1) and P-glycoprotein efflux pump. Treatment for advanced RCC instead relies on targeted therapies (TKIs targeting VEGF pathway such as sunitinib, and mTOR inhibitors) and immune checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab/ipilimumab). The VEGF pathway is particularly important as a therapeutic target because VHL gene loss drives VEGF overexpression in clear cell RCC.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: primary pathway to be targeted includes the VEGF pathway due to VHL tumor suppressor gene loss

stem: A 50-year-old woman leaks urine with coughing, sneezing, and exercise. Physical exam shows urethral hypermobility on Q-tip test. What type of incontinence is this?

choices.feedback: Correct! SUI: involuntary leakage with increased abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, laugh, exercise, lifting). Mechanism: weakened pelvic floor support → urethral hypermobility → inadequate urethral closure during stress. Risk factors: vaginal delivery, obesity, menopause (estrogen deficiency), chronic cough, pelvic surgery. Q-tip test: cotton swab in urethra → straining deflects >30° = hypermobility. Treatment ladder: pelvic floor exercises (Kegel) → pessary → midurethral sling (TVT/TOT — gold standard surgical).
Functional incontinence occurs when physical or cognitive impairment prevents reaching the toilet in time — the urinary tract is normal.
Overflow incontinence results from incomplete emptying (large postvoid residual) with continuous dribbling. Not triggered by coughing.
Urge incontinence is leaking associated with a sudden strong desire to void — not triggered by physical exertion.

explanation:
SUI: involuntary leakage with increased abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, laugh, exercise, lifting). Mechanism: weakened pelvic floor support → urethral hypermobility → inadequate urethral closure during stress. Risk factors: vaginal delivery, obesity, menopause (estrogen deficiency), chronic cough, pelvic surgery. Q-tip test: cotton swab in urethra → straining deflects >30° = hypermobility. stem: A 65-year-old woman reports sudden urgency and leaking before she can reach the bathroom, occurring multiple times daily. Postvoid residual is low. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Sling surgery treats stress incontinence, not urge incontinence. OAB requires different management.
CIC is for retention/incomplete emptying (overflow). This patient has low PVR — her bladder empties fine; it just contracts involuntarily.

Correct! Urge incontinence (overactive bladder — OAB): first-line = behavioral therapy: bladder training (timed voiding, gradually increasing intervals), pelvic floor exercises, fluid management, and lifestyle modifications. If insufficient: second-line = antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin) or beta-3 agonist (mirabegron). Third-line: onabotulinumtoxin A injection into detrusor, sacral neuromodulation (InterStim), or posterior tibial nerve stimulation.
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) treat benign prostatic hyperplasia in men. They are not indicated for female OAB.

verifiedExplanation: This presentation describes urge incontinence — sudden, intense urge to void with involuntary leakage before reaching the bathroom. It is caused by detrusor overactivity (involuntary bladder contractions during filling). Treatment starts with behavioral therapy (bladder retraining) and antimuscarinics. Unlike stress incontinence (which occurs with physical exertion), urge incontinence involves the sudden and uncontrollable need to void. Urodynamic studies can confirm detrusor overactivity if the diagnosis is unclear.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: urge incontinence from detrusor overactivity

stem: What is the gold standard surgical treatment for female stress urinary incontinence?

choices.feedback: Sacral neuromodulation (InterStim) treats OAB/urge incontinence and refractory voiding dysfunction, not primary SUI.
Botox injection into the detrusor treats OAB (urge incontinence), not stress incontinence.
Augmentation is for neurogenic bladders with poor compliance, not stress incontinence.

Correct! Midurethral slings: polypropylene mesh placed under the mid-urethra. TVT (tension-free vaginal tape — retropubic) or TOT (transobturator tape). Success rates: 80-90% cure/improvement. Mechanism: supports the urethra during stress → prevents hypermobility → maintains closure pressure. Complications: mesh erosion, voiding dysfunction, pain. Alternatives: Burch colposuspension (laparoscopic), urethral bulking agents (less durable), pubovaginal sling (autologous fascia — for recurrence or complicated cases).

verifiedExplanation: The midurethral sling (tension-free vaginal tape/TVT or transobturator tape/TOT) is the gold standard surgical treatment for female stress urinary incontinence. It provides a backboard of support beneath the midurethra, reinforcing the pubourethral ligament mechanism. Success rates exceed 80-90% at long-term follow-up. Surgery is reserved for patients who fail conservative measures (pelvic floor exercises, pessary). Complications include mesh erosion, voiding dysfunction, and bladder perforation.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: midurethral sling as gold standard for surgical SUI treatment

stem: Mixed urinary incontinence in a woman involves which combination?

choices.feedback: Day vs night timing does not define mixed incontinence. The distinction is between stress and urge mechanisms.
Continuous leakage suggests fistula or overflow. Mixed incontinence is specifically stress + urge.

choices.text: Both stress and urge incontinence symptoms
choices.feedback: Correct! Mixed incontinence: patient experiences BOTH stress leakage (with cough/exercise) AND urgency with leaking (before reaching toilet). Very common — affects up to 30% of incontinent women. Management: treat the predominant/most bothersome component first. If urge predominant → behavioral therapy + anticholinergics. If stress predominant → pelvic floor therapy → consider sling. Often requires combination approach addressing both components.
Mixed incontinence specifically refers to the combination of stress and urge components.

verifiedExplanation: Mixed urinary incontinence involves the combination of both stress incontinence (leakage with exertion, coughing, sneezing) and urge incontinence (sudden urgency with leakage). It is common and represents the coexistence of urethral hypermobility/sphincter deficiency with detrusor overactivity. Treatment addresses the predominant symptom first — if urgency is more bothersome, start with behavioral therapy and antimuscarinics; if stress symptoms predominate, consider pelvic floor training and potentially surgical intervention.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: mixed incontinence combines stress and urge components

stem: An elderly man in a nursing home with dementia is found wet multiple times daily. He can walk independently. What type of incontinence is most likely?

choices.feedback: BPH-related overflow is possible but would present with palpable bladder, dribbling, and elevated PVR. The dementia context makes functional incontinence most likely.

Correct! Functional incontinence: the urinary tract is normal, but cognitive or physical impairment prevents appropriate toileting. In dementia: patient does not recognize the urge or cannot navigate to the bathroom despite being physically capable. Management: prompted voiding (caregiver-initiated toileting schedule), environmental modifications (clear path to bathroom, bedside commode, nightlights), timed voiding (regular intervals), and minimizing contributing factors (diuretics, fluid timing). Medications and surgery are NOT appropriate.
While urgency can coexist, the clinical picture of cognitive impairment with an otherwise mobile patient most suggests functional incontinence.
Stress incontinence is less common in men (usually post-prostatectomy). Dementia-related incontinence has a different mechanism.

verifiedExplanation: This scenario describes functional incontinence — urinary leakage in a patient with normal lower urinary tract function who cannot reach the toilet due to cognitive impairment (dementia), physical disability, or environmental barriers. The bladder and sphincter work normally; the problem is the patient's inability to recognize the need to void or physically access the bathroom. Management focuses on prompted voiding schedules, caregiver assistance, accessible toileting, and addressing modifiable barriers.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: functional incontinence from inability to reach toilet due to cognitive or physical impairment

stem: During male fetal development, what hormone produced by Sertoli cells causes regression of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts?

choices.feedback: DHT (converted from testosterone by 5α-reductase) masculinizes the external genitalia (penis, scrotum, prostate). It does not cause Müllerian regression.

Correct! Sertoli cells produce AMH (also called Müllerian inhibiting substance/MIS) → causes regression of the paramesonephric (Müllerian) ducts (which would otherwise develop into uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina). Leydig cells produce testosterone → drives mesonephric (Wolffian) duct development AND (via 5α-reductase → DHT) external genitalia masculinization. Both hormones are required for normal male development; deficiency of either causes specific DSD phenotypes.
Testosterone (from Leydig cells) drives Wolffian (mesonephric) duct differentiation into vas deferens, epididymis, and seminal vesicles. A different hormone regresses the Müllerian structures.
Estrogen does not cause Müllerian regression. In fact, Müllerian structures develop into female reproductive organs under estrogen influence in the absence of AMH.

explanation: Correct: Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH/MIS).
verifiedExplanation: Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), produced by Sertoli cells during male fetal development, causes regression of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts. Without AMH, Müllerian ducts would develop into the fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper vagina. This is why persistent Müllerian duct syndrome (failure of AMH production or receptor defect) results in a phenotypic male with cryptorchidism who is found to have a uterus and fallopian tubes at surgery.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: Sertoli cells produce AMH causing Müllerian duct regression

stem: A phenotypic female at puberty presents with primary amenorrhea, absent uterus on imaging, and 46,XY karyotype. Testosterone levels are high. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: 5α-reductase deficiency causes ambiguous genitalia at birth with virilization at puberty (when testosterone rises). Not a completely female phenotype.

Correct! CAIS: 46,XY with nonfunctional androgen receptors → external phenotype is completely female (because DHT cannot act on external genitalia). Testes are present (produce testosterone — hence high levels — and AMH). AMH → Müllerian regression → absent uterus/tubes/upper vagina. Testes often in inguinal canal or labia. Presents at puberty with primary amenorrhea and breast development (testosterone aromatized to estrogen). Management: gonadectomy after puberty (cancer risk), estrogen replacement, vaginal dilators or vaginoplasty.
Turner syndrome is 45,X with streak gonads and female phenotype but would NOT have 46,XY karyotype or high testosterone.
CAH in a 46,XY individual would cause virilization, not a female phenotype. CAH causes virilization in 46,XX females.

verifiedExplanation: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) presents as a phenotypic female who presents at puberty with primary amenorrhea, absent uterus (because Sertoli cells still produce AMH), and bilateral inguinal masses (testes). The karyotype is 46,XY. Despite normal or elevated testosterone, the androgen receptor is nonfunctional, so Wolffian duct structures fail to develop and external genitalia feminize by default. Breast development occurs through peripheral aromatization of testosterone to estrogen. No pubic or axillary hair develops due to androgen insensitivity.

stem: The ureteric bud arises from which embryologic structure?

choices.feedback: The paramesonephric duct gives rise to the uterus, fallopian tubes, and upper vagina — not the urinary collecting system.

Correct! The ureteric bud branches off the caudal mesonephric (Wolffian) duct during week 5 → grows toward the metanephric mesenchyme (metanephric blastema) → reciprocal induction → ureteric bud forms the collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter) while the metanephric mesenchyme forms the nephron (glomerulus through DCT). Abnormal ureteric bud position → abnormal ureteral insertion → VUR, ectopia, duplication anomalies.
The cloaca divides into the urogenital sinus (anterior) and anorectal canal (posterior). The ureteric bud arises from the mesonephric duct, not the cloaca directly.
The allantois contributes to the urachus (median umbilical ligament) and part of the bladder dome, not the ureteric bud.

The ureteric bud branches off the caudal mesonephric (Wolffian) duct during week 5 → grows toward the metanephric mesenchyme (metanephric blastema) → reciprocal induction → ureteric bud forms the collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter) while the metanephric mesenchyme forms the nephron (glomerulus through DCT). Abnormal ureteric bud position → abnormal ureteral insertion → VUR, ectopia, duplication anomalies.

stem: What is the embryologic origin of the kidney's functional nephron (glomerulus through distal convoluted tubule)?

choices.feedback: The ureteric bud forms the COLLECTING system (collecting ducts, calyces, pelvis, ureter) — not the nephron itself.
While intermediate mesoderm gives rise to the urogenital ridge, the specific nephron-forming tissue is the metanephric mesenchyme within the intermediate mesoderm.
Neural crest cells contribute to the adrenal medulla, peripheral nervous system, and melanocytes — not the nephron.

Correct! Reciprocal induction: ureteric bud signals → metanephric mesenchyme condenses and differentiates → forms the nephron (Bowman's capsule, PCT, loop of Henle, DCT). Metanephric mesenchyme signals → ureteric bud branches repeatedly → forms collecting system. Failure of this induction → renal agenesis. The permanent kidney (metanephros) begins forming in week 5 and is functional by week 10-12. Earlier kidneys: pronephros (week 4, degenerates) → mesonephros (temporary, becomes Wolffian duct structures in males).

explanation: Correct: Metanephric mesenchyme (metanephric blastema).
verifiedExplanation: The metanephric mesenchyme (metanephric blastema) gives rise to the functional nephron components: glomerulus, Bowman capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, and distal convoluted tubule. This occurs through reciprocal induction with the ureteric bud — the ureteric bud induces the mesenchyme to undergo mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition and form nephrons, while the mesenchyme signals back to promote ureteric bud branching. The collecting system (collecting ducts through ureter) derives from the ureteric bud, not the mesenchyme.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: metanephric mesenchyme forms the functional nephron through reciprocal induction

stem: What structure does the gubernaculum guide during testicular descent?

choices.text: The testis from the abdomen through the inguinal canal to the scrotum
choices.feedback: Correct! The gubernaculum is a fibrous cord connecting the inferior pole of the testis to the future scrotum. Testicular descent occurs in two phases: (1) Transabdominal (weeks 8-15): AMH/MIS causes gubernacular swelling → holds testis near the internal ring while the abdomen elongates. (2) Inguinoscrotal (weeks 25-35): androgens (testosterone via CGRP from genitofemoral nerve) → gubernacular regression/shortening → guides testis through inguinal canal into scrotum. The processus vaginalis (peritoneal evagination) precedes the testis.

choices.text: The spermatic cord through the deep inguinal ring
choices.feedback: The spermatic cord forms as the testis descends, but the gubernaculum is the primary guide.

choices.text: The epididymis from the mesonephros to the testis
choices.feedback: The epididymis derives from mesonephric tubules and duct, not guided by the gubernaculum.

choices.text: The Müllerian duct toward the pelvis
choices.feedback: The gubernaculum guides testicular descent. In males, the Müllerian duct regresses due to AMH.

verifiedExplanation: The gubernaculum guides the testis during its descent from the abdomen through the inguinal canal into the scrotum. Testicular descent occurs in two phases: the transabdominal phase (weeks 10-15, regulated by insulin-like factor 3/INSL3 from Leydig cells acting on the gubernaculum) and the inguinoscrotal phase (weeks 25-35, regulated by androgens and the genitofemoral nerve/CGRP). The processus vaginalis extends along the gubernaculum's path; failure of its obliteration results in an indirect inguinal hernia or communicating hydrocele.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: gubernaculum guides testicular descent through the inguinal canal

stem: A newborn boy presents with bilateral non-palpable testes and penoscrotal hypospadias. What evaluation is most important?

choices.feedback: While renal ultrasound may be part of the workup, bilateral non-palpable testes + severe hypospadias requires DSD evaluation first.

Correct! Bilateral non-palpable testes + hypospadias = possible disorder of sex development (DSD). Must rule out: congenital adrenal hyperplasia (46,XX DSD — virilized female; potentially life-threatening salt-wasting crisis). Urgent: karyotype, 17-OHP (21-hydroxylase deficiency), electrolytes (hyponatremia/hyperkalemia = salt-wasting CAH). Also: pelvic ultrasound (uterus?), gonadal evaluation, testosterone/DHT levels. Do NOT assign gender until evaluation complete. CAH is the most common cause of ambiguous genitalia in 46,XX individuals.
Hormonal stimulation testing may be part of the workup but karyotype and CAH screening take priority.
Surgical correction should NOT proceed until DSD evaluation is complete. The gonads may be ovaries or ovotestes.

explanation: Correct: Karyotype and DSD workup (including 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes, cortisol).
verifiedExplanation: Bilateral non-palpable testes with penoscrotal hypospadias in a newborn requires urgent karyotype and DSD (disorder of sex development) workup. This presentation could represent a virilized 46,XX individual (e.g., congenital adrenal hyperplasia with salt-wasting), which is a medical emergency requiring immediate electrolyte monitoring and cortisol replacement. Initial evaluation includes karyotype, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes, pelvic ultrasound, and endocrinology consultation. Gender assignment should be deferred pending results.

stem: Anti-Müllerian Hormone (AMH) is produced by which cells and causes regression of which structure?

choices.text: Gonadotrophs; regression of both duct systems
choices.feedback: Gonadotrophs are pituitary cells. AMH is produced locally in the gonad by Sertoli cells and specifically targets Müllerian ducts only.

choices.text: Theca cells; regression of mesonephric duct
choices.feedback: Theca cells are ovarian cells that produce androgens for estrogen synthesis. AMH is a testicular Sertoli cell product.

choices.text: Leydig cells; regression of Wolffian duct
choices.feedback: Leydig cells produce testosterone (which maintains the Wolffian duct). AMH comes from a different cell type.

choices.text: Sertoli cells; regression of Müllerian (paramesonephric) ducts
choices.feedback: Correct! Sertoli cells in the fetal testis produce AMH (also called Müllerian Inhibiting Substance/MIS) → causes apoptosis and regression of the Müllerian ducts (which would otherwise develop into uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina). This occurs by 8-10 weeks. In females: no AMH → Müllerian ducts persist → develop into internal female structures. Persistent Müllerian duct syndrome: males with AMH gene or AMH receptor mutations → retain uterus/fallopian tubes despite normal external virilization.

stem: 5-alpha reductase deficiency presents at birth as which phenotype?

choices.text: Ambiguous genitalia at birth with virilization at puberty
choices.feedback: Correct! 5α-reductase type 2 deficiency: 46,XY individuals cannot convert testosterone → DHT. DHT is required for: external male genitalia development (penile growth, scrotal fusion, prostate). Testosterone still works for: Wolffian duct development (epididymis, vas, seminal vesicles) and pubertal virilization. Birth: ambiguous/female-appearing external genitalia, bilateral testes (inguinal or labial). Puberty: testosterone surge → virilization (voice deepening, muscle mass, penile growth — but no prostate enlargement). Often raised female, then virilize at puberty ('guevedoces' in Dominican Republic).
External male genitalia require DHT. Without 5α-reductase, external genitalia are undervirilized at birth.

choices.text: Normal female external genitalia with absent uterus
choices.feedback: Absent uterus with female external genitalia describes Complete Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (CAIS), not 5-alpha reductase deficiency.

choices.text: Bilateral streak gonads with Turner phenotype
choices.feedback: Streak gonads occur in Turner syndrome (45,X) or gonadal dysgenesis. 5α-reductase deficiency has normal testes.

explanation: Correct: Ambiguous genitalia at birth with virilization at puberty (46,XY).
verifiedExplanation: 5-alpha reductase deficiency presents at birth with ambiguous genitalia (micropenis, hypospadias, bifid scrotum) in a 46,XY individual. At puberty, the surge in testosterone causes virilization: voice deepening, muscle mass increase, phallic enlargement, and testicular descent — without requiring DHT conversion. These individuals are sometimes raised as female and undergo virilization at puberty (historically described in the Dominican Republic as 'güevedoces'). Wolffian structures develop normally because testosterone (not DHT) drives their formation.

stem: At what age should surgical orchiopexy be performed for a persistently undescended testis?

choices.feedback: 3-5 years is too late. Germ cell damage is already significant by this age in cryptorchid testes.
Waiting until puberty significantly increases risk of infertility and testicular cancer.
Orchiopexy is indicated for ALL undescended testes — unilateral or bilateral — due to fertility and malignancy risks.

Correct! AUA guidelines: orchiopexy by 6-12 months (adjusted gestational age). Spontaneous descent rarely occurs after 6 months. Early surgery preserves fertility potential (germ cell deterioration begins by 12-18 months in undescended testes) and allows tumor surveillance. Risk of testicular cancer is 4-8× higher in cryptorchid testes — orchiopexy reduces but does not eliminate this risk.

Correct Answer: By 6-12 months of age

By 6-12 months of age (no later than 18 months).

stem: What is the most common testicular tumor arising in a cryptorchid testis?

choices.feedback: Teratomas occur in undescended testes but seminoma is the most common malignancy.

Correct! Seminoma is the most common malignancy in cryptorchid testes. Risk is 4-8× the general population. Intra-abdominal testes have higher risk than inguinal. stem: How is a retractile testis distinguished from a truly undescended testis?

choices.feedback: Volume can help assess atrophy but is not the primary distinguishing exam.
MRI is reserved for nonpalpable testes. Physical exam (gentle manipulation) is sufficient to distinguish retractile from undescended.
hCG stimulation tests for testosterone response (confirms testicular tissue exists) but does not distinguish retractile from undescended.

choices.text: A retractile testis can be manually brought into the scrotum
choices.feedback: Correct! Retractile testis: hyperactive cremasteric reflex pulls a normally descended testis into the inguinal canal. Key finding: can be manually manipulated into the scrotum AND stays there (at least briefly) without tension. No surgery needed — follow annually until puberty (when cremaster weakens). Truly undescended: cannot reach the scrotum or immediately retracts back when released. Ascending testis: previously scrotal testis that becomes undescended (requires orchiopexy).

stem: What is the initial diagnostic approach for a nonpalpable undescended testis?

choices.feedback: Correct! stem: What is the recommended approach for a nonpalpable testis found high intra-abdominally on laparoscopy?

choices.text: Staged Fowler-Stephens orchiopexy or orchiectomy if the testis appears atrophic
choices.feedback: Correct! High intra-abdominal testis options: Fowler-Stephens orchiopexy (staged): ligate testicular vessels (testis survives on collateral vasal/cremasteric vessels) → second-stage mobilization to scrotum 6 months later. Success rate ~85%. Orchiectomy if: testis is severely atrophic/dysplastic, contralateral testis is normal, or patient is postpubertal. Decision factors: age, testicular appearance, contralateral status.
Observation leaves the patient at risk for malignancy in an unmonitored intra-abdominal testis.
High intra-abdominal testes cannot reach the scrotum in a single-stage standard orchiopexy due to insufficient vessel length. Staged Fowler-Stephens is needed.
Hormonal therapy success rate is very low (~20%) and is not recommended for truly nonpalpable intra-abdominal testes. Surgery is required.

verifiedExplanation: For a nonpalpable testis found high intra-abdominally on laparoscopy, a staged Fowler-Stephens orchiopexy is the recommended approach. Stage 1 involves clipping the testicular (spermatic) vessels to allow collateral blood supply to develop via the vasal artery and cremasteric vessels. Stage 2 (typically 6 months later) involves mobilizing the testis into the scrotum on its collateral blood supply. If the testis appears atrophic or nubbin-like on laparoscopy, orchiectomy is performed because an atrophic intra-abdominal testis carries malignancy risk without functional benefit.

stem: What is the most important preoperative instruction for an infant with hypospadias?

choices.text: Start topical testosterone to promote penile growth
choices.feedback: Preoperative topical testosterone (or DHT) may be used for severe cases with a small phallus to increase tissue for repair, but the critical instruction is avoiding circumcision.

choices.text: Do NOT circumcise — the foreskin is needed for surgical reconstruction
choices.feedback: Correct! The foreskin (prepuce) is the primary tissue source for hypospadias repair (onlay island flap, tubularized incised plate). Chordee (ventral curvature) is corrected simultaneously. Complications: urethrocutaneous fistula (most common), meatal stenosis, urethral stricture.

stem: What embryologic event leads to hypospadias?

choices.feedback: While exogenous estrogens have been studied, the direct mechanism is insufficient DHT-mediated urethral fold fusion, not excess estrogen.

stem: What is the primary goal of neurogenic bladder management in children with spina bifida?

choices.text: Maintain low bladder storage pressures to protect the upper urinary tract
choices.feedback: Correct! Primary goal: keep detrusor pressures < 40 cm H2O to prevent vesicoureteral reflux → hydronephrosis → renal damage. Strategy: CIC (clean intermittent catheterization) every 3-4 hours + anticholinergics (oxybutynin) to relax the detrusor. Monitor: renal ultrasound annually, urodynamics periodically. Surgical options if medical management fails: bladder augmentation (enterocystoplasty), continent catheterizable channel (Mitrofanoff — appendicovesicostomy).

choices.text: Minimize the number of daily catheterizations
choices.feedback: The goal is not minimizing catheterizations — it's maintaining low pressures. Inadequate catheterization frequency leads to high-pressure storage and upper tract damage.
Most children with spina bifida cannot achieve normal volitional voiding. Management focuses on protecting the kidneys.
While UTIs should be treated, some degree of bacteriuria is common with CIC and does not require treatment (asymptomatic bacteriuria). Upper tract protection is the primary goal.

stem: Urodynamic study in a child with myelomeningocele shows detrusor pressure of 55 cm H2O during filling. What does this indicate?

choices.text: Poor bladder compliance with risk of upper tract damage
choices.feedback: Correct! Detrusor pressure >40 cm H2O during storage = hostile bladder → vesicoureteral reflux, hydronephrosis, and progressive renal damage. Immediate management: start/optimize CIC + anticholinergics (oxybutynin or tolterodine). If refractory: botulinum toxin injection into detrusor wall, or surgical bladder augmentation. The 40 cm H2O threshold (McGuire 'leak point pressure') is the critical cutoff for upper tract risk.
Medical management (CIC + anticholinergics) should be optimized first. Surgery is reserved for refractory cases.
Normal detrusor pressure during filling is well below 40 cm H2O. 55 cm H2O indicates dangerously high pressure.

choices.text: Voluntary detrusor contraction — good prognostic sign
choices.feedback: In neurogenic bladder, high pressures during filling indicate poor compliance and involuntary detrusor overactivity — not a good sign.

explanation: Key detail: requires intervention.
verifiedExplanation: A urodynamic study showing detrusor pressure of 55 cm H₂O indicates poor bladder compliance with significant risk of upper tract damage. The critical threshold is 40 cm H₂O — pressures above this are transmitted to the kidneys via reflux and cause progressive hydronephrosis and renal deterioration. This child needs aggressive intervention: start CIC if not already doing so, optimize anticholinergic therapy, and closely monitor upper tracts with renal ultrasound and serum creatinine.

stem: What is a Mitrofanoff procedure?

choices.text: Creation of a continent catheterizable channel (often using appendix) from bladder to skin
choices.feedback: Correct! Mitrofanoff appendicovesicostomy: the appendix is reimplanted into the bladder (with a non-refluxing tunnel) and brought to the skin (usually umbilicus) → continent catheterizable stoma. Allows CIC through a channel that is socially discreet (umbilical stoma), accessible (wheelchair users can self-catheterize), and continent between catheterizations. If appendix unavailable: Monti channel (reconfigured ileal segment). Often combined with bladder augmentation.

choices.text: Bladder neck reconstruction to achieve continence
choices.feedback: Bladder neck procedures address the outlet, not catheterization access.
AUS addresses incontinence at the outlet level. Mitrofanoff creates a catheterizable access channel to the bladder.
Mitrofanoff is a continent channel that avoids permanent indwelling catheters. Patients catheterize intermittently through the channel.

verifiedExplanation: A Mitrofanoff procedure creates a continent catheterizable channel using the appendix (or rarely a tapered ileal segment) connected from the bladder to a stoma on the abdominal wall, typically at the umbilicus. This allows clean intermittent catheterization through the abdominal wall rather than the urethra, which is particularly important for patients with impaired hand function, wheelchair dependence, or urethral pathology. The channel has a flap-valve mechanism that maintains continence between catheterizations.

stem: Which medication is first-line for reducing detrusor overactivity in neurogenic bladder?

choices.feedback: Baclofen (GABA-B agonist) reduces skeletal muscle spasticity, not detrusor smooth muscle overactivity.

Correct! Oxybutynin (M3 muscarinic antagonist): blocks acetylcholine-mediated detrusor contractions → reduces uninhibited detrusor overactivity → increases bladder capacity and compliance → lowers storage pressures. Available: oral, transdermal patch (fewer side effects), intravesical instillation (direct bladder, minimal systemic absorption). Side effects: dry mouth, constipation, cognitive effects (especially elderly), heat intolerance. Alternatives: tolterodine, solifenacin, mirabegron (β3 agonist — different mechanism).
DDAVP reduces urine production (antidiuretic) and is used for nocturnal enuresis, not detrusor overactivity.
Alpha-blockers relax the bladder outlet/prostate (used in BPH). They do not reduce detrusor overactivity.

explanation: Correct: Anticholinergics (oxybutynin).
verifiedExplanation: Anticholinergics (particularly oxybutynin) are the first-line pharmacologic treatment for reducing detrusor overactivity in neurogenic bladder. They block muscarinic receptors on the detrusor muscle, reducing uninhibited contractions and improving bladder compliance. This lowers storage pressures and protects the upper urinary tract. Common side effects include dry mouth, constipation, and facial flushing. In children, intravesical oxybutynin instillation can reduce systemic side effects while maintaining local efficacy.

stem: What is the most common cause of lower urinary tract obstruction in male neonates?

choices.feedback: Physiologic phimosis is normal in neonates and does not cause urinary tract obstruction.
Congenital urethral strictures are rare. PUV is far more common.

Correct! PUV is the most common cause of congenital lower urinary tract obstruction in males. Mucosal folds in the posterior urethra (Type I most common — folds from verumontanum to anterior urethral wall) obstruct urine flow. Prenatal ultrasound: bilateral hydronephrosis, distended bladder, dilated posterior urethra ('keyhole sign'), oligohydramnios (severe cases). Can cause renal dysplasia, pulmonary hypoplasia (Potter sequence). Treatment: catheter drainage → cystoscopic valve ablation.
Meatal stenosis is usually acquired (post-circumcision) and rarely causes significant obstruction in neonates.

verifiedExplanation: Posterior urethral valves (PUV) are the most common cause of lower urinary tract obstruction in male neonates. They consist of abnormal membranous folds in the posterior urethra, classified by Young's criteria into three types. Type I (95% of cases) involves folds arising from the caudal verumontanum that fuse anteriorly. PUV occurs exclusively in males. The reported incidence ranges from 1 in 8,000 to 1 in 25,000 live births. The degree of obstruction determines clinical severity, ranging from asymptomatic to incompatible with life.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: most common cause of urinary tract obstruction and chronic kidney disease resulting from obstructive uropathy in the pediatric population

stem: An infant with PUV has bilateral hydronephrosis and a creatinine of 1.5 mg/dL at 1 month. What is the first management step?

choices.text: Urethral catheter drainage to decompress the system, followed by valve ablation
choices.feedback: Correct! Immediate management: (1) Foley catheter drainage → decompresses bladder and upper tracts, allows creatinine to nadir (nadir creatinine is the best predictor of long-term renal function). (2) Monitor electrolytes (post-obstructive diuresis can cause massive salt/water losses). (3) Renal ultrasound to assess hydronephrosis response. (4) Definitive: cystoscopic primary valve ablation (PVA) once infant is stable. If catheter alone doesn't decompress: vesicostomy or high diversion.
Creatinine of 1.5 in a 1-month-old with bilateral hydronephrosis indicates significant obstruction. Immediate drainage is required.
Nephrostomy is reserved for cases where bladder catheter drainage fails to decompress the upper tracts. Start with urethral catheter.
While valve ablation is definitive, the immediate priority is drainage to stabilize renal function.

verifiedExplanation: For an infant with PUV, bilateral hydronephrosis, and elevated creatinine, the immediate priority is urethral catheter drainage to decompress the obstructed system, followed by definitive valve ablation once the child is stabilized. Catheter decompression allows assessment of renal recoverability — the nadir creatinine after decompression is the best predictor of long-term renal function. Persistent elevation above 1.0 mg/dL after decompression suggests significant underlying renal dysplasia and a higher risk of progression to ESRD.

stem: What long-term complication are PUV patients at highest risk for?

choices.feedback: While urethral stricture can occur, CKD/ESRD is the most significant long-term risk.

choices.text: Chronic kidney disease progressing to ESRD
choices.feedback: Correct! ~30% of PUV patients develop ESRD by adolescence despite early valve ablation. Nadir creatinine <0.8 mg/dL: best prognosis. Nadir >1.0 mg/dL: high ESRD risk. Lifelong monitoring: renal function, blood pressure, urodynamics, bladder management.
PUV does not significantly increase bladder cancer risk.
PUV does not increase testicular torsion risk.

Associated findings: bilateral hydronephrosis, oligohydramnios (decreased amniotic fluid from reduced fetal urine output), and in severe cases signs consistent with pulmonary hypoplasia. Prenatal intervention: vesicoamniotic shunting may be considered in severe cases with oligohydramnios threatening lung development.

stem: What is 'valve bladder syndrome'?

choices.text: Bladder stones forming on residual valve tissue
choices.feedback: Stones are not a feature of valve bladder syndrome. The syndrome is about persistent myogenic bladder dysfunction.

choices.text: Vesicoureteral reflux caused by valve obstruction
choices.feedback: While VUR is common with PUV, valve bladder syndrome specifically refers to the persistent bladder dysfunction (compliance/emptying) that continues after valve ablation.

choices.text: Persistent bladder dysfunction (poor compliance, detrusor overactivity) despite successful valve ablation
choices.feedback: Correct! Valve bladder syndrome: the bladder develops abnormal compliance, overactivity, and poor emptying due to prolonged in-utero/neonatal obstruction — these changes persist even after valve ablation. Presents as: high storage pressures, incontinence, incomplete emptying, recurrent UTIs, ongoing upper tract damage. Management: CIC ± anticholinergics (similar to neurogenic bladder), bladder augmentation if refractory. Affects up to 50% of PUV patients long-term.

choices.text: Recurrence of urethral valves after ablation
choices.feedback: Valves do not regrow after successful ablation. Valve bladder syndrome refers to persistent bladder dysfunction.

verifiedExplanation: 'Valve bladder syndrome' refers to persistent bladder dysfunction (poor compliance, detrusor overactivity, incomplete emptying) that continues despite successful endoscopic valve ablation. It results from the chronic in-utero and postnatal effects of high-pressure obstruction on the developing detrusor muscle, which undergoes fibrosis and smooth muscle hypertrophy. Valve bladder syndrome contributes to ongoing upper tract deterioration even after obstruction is relieved and is a major reason PUV patients require lifelong urologic follow-up. Management may include clean intermittent catheterization and anticholinergic medications, with augmentation cystoplasty reserved for refractory cases.

stem: What is the classic triad of renal cell carcinoma presentation?

choices.feedback: Dysuria and frequency suggest UTI/cystitis. The classic RCC triad involves different findings.
These are B symptoms seen in lymphoma. While RCC can cause fever and weight loss, the classic triad is hematuria, flank pain, and mass.
This suggests bilateral renal obstruction or nephrotic/nephritic syndrome, not RCC.

choices.feedback: Correct! Classic triad (seen in <10% of patients at diagnosis): painless hematuria (most common single symptom), flank pain, palpable mass. 'Internist's tumor' — presents with diverse systemic symptoms.

stem: A 55-year-old man has an incidentally discovered 3 cm enhancing renal mass on CT. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Correct! AUA guidelines: for T1a renal masses (≤4 cm), partial nephrectomy is preferred over radical nephrectomy when technically feasible. Surveillance (active surveillance) is appropriate for small masses in elderly/comorbid patients. Biopsy role: growing, indeterminate masses, or when changing management.
Radical nephrectomy (removing entire kidney) is reserved for larger tumors (T1b-T2+) or when partial nephrectomy is not technically feasible.
Biopsy is not routinely required for solid enhancing renal masses — imaging characteristics are usually sufficient.

stem: Which genetic syndrome is most associated with bilateral/multifocal clear cell renal cell carcinoma?

stem: RCC classically metastasizes to which unusual site, and what is its hallmark vascular behavior?

choices.feedback: Correct! RCC is one of few tumors that grows directly into veins: renal vein → IVC → right atrium (tumor thrombus, not blood clot). IVC thrombus occurs in ~10% and requires surgical thrombectomy. Metastatic sites: lung (most common), bone (lytic — highly vascular, bleed during biopsy), brain, liver, adrenal. RCC is hypervascular → hemorrhagic metastases (classic: bleeding brain met, solitary lytic bone lesion).
Axillary nodal spread is characteristic of breast cancer. RCC spreads to para-aortic/retroperitoneal nodes and hematogenously.
While RCC can metastasize to the liver, this is not its unique metastatic pattern.
Peritoneal seeding is characteristic of ovarian and GI cancers. RCC characteristically spreads hematogenously, especially via venous extension.

explanation: Best answer: Extends into the renal vein and IVC (tumor thrombus), and metastasizes to bone and lung.
verifiedExplanation: RCC has a hallmark tendency to extend into the renal vein and inferior vena cava as a tumor thrombus, which occurs in approximately 4-10% of cases. This venous extension can propagate into the right atrium. RCC also metastasizes to unusual sites including lung (most common, ~75%), bone, liver, brain, and contralateral kidney. The pattern of distant spread reflects RCC's highly vascular nature. Cannonball metastases in the lungs are a classic radiographic finding.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: renal vein involvement and IVC tumor thrombus extension

stem: What is Stauffer syndrome in the context of RCC?

choices.feedback: Ectopic ACTH production is rare in RCC. Stauffer syndrome specifically refers to the hepatic dysfunction.
Hypercalcemia is a separate RCC paraneoplastic syndrome (PTHrP production). Stauffer syndrome involves hepatic dysfunction.

choices.text: Nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction (elevated ALP, transaminases) that resolves after nephrectomy
choices.feedback: Correct! Stauffer syndrome: paraneoplastic hepatopathy — elevated alkaline phosphatase, transaminases, prolonged PT, hepatosplenomegaly WITHOUT liver metastases. Caused by IL-6 production by the tumor. Key feature: resolves completely after nephrectomy (recurrence suggests tumor recurrence). Do not mistake for liver metastases — biopsy shows nonspecific inflammation, not tumor. Occurs in 3-20% of RCC patients.
EPO-mediated erythrocytosis is a different RCC paraneoplastic syndrome.

verifiedExplanation: Stauffer syndrome is a paraneoplastic nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction associated with RCC. It presents with elevated alkaline phosphatase, transaminases, and prolonged PT/INR in the absence of liver metastases. The syndrome is mediated by tumor-secreted cytokines, particularly IL-6. The hallmark feature is that hepatic function normalizes completely after nephrectomy. Recurrence of Stauffer syndrome after initial resolution should raise concern for tumor recurrence. It occurs in approximately 3-20% of RCC patients.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: Stauffer syndrome - nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction that resolves after nephrectomy

stem: A woman leaks urine when coughing, sneezing, or laughing. What type of incontinence is this?

choices.feedback: Urge incontinence is involuntary leakage accompanied by a strong urge to void (overactive bladder). Activity-related leakage without urgency is different.

stem: What is the first-line treatment for stress urinary incontinence?

choices.text: Pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises) with or without biofeedback
choices.feedback: Correct! Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT): strengthens the levator ani → improves urethral support and sphincter function. stem: What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for urge incontinence (overactive bladder)?

stem: What is the most common cause of prenatal hydronephrosis?

stem: What nuclear medicine study is used to evaluate UPJ obstruction and differential renal function?

choices.feedback: DMSA evaluates renal cortical scarring and relative function but does NOT assess drainage (not dynamic). MAG3 with diuretic is preferred for obstruction.
