choices.text: International Reflux Grading (I-V): Grades IV-V have the lowest resolution rates
choices.feedback: Correct! International Reflux Grading (VCUG): I = ureter only, II = ureter + pelvis (no dilation), III = mild ureteral/pelvic dilation + mild calyceal blunting, IV = moderate dilation + calyceal blunting, V = massive dilation + tortuous ureter + complete calyceal effacement. Resolution rates: Grades I-II = ~80-90%, Grade III = ~50%, Grades IV-V = <20%. Higher grades → earlier surgical referral. Bilateral > unilateral risk for scarring.

choices.text: Grades A-D based on ultrasound; Grade D worst
choices.feedback: VUR uses International Reflux Grading (I-V) based on VCUG, not lettered grades based on ultrasound.

explanation: The International Reflux Grading System (I-V) is based on VCUG findings. Grades IV-V have the lowest spontaneous resolution rates (~10-20% for Grade IV, <5% for Grade V) due to severe ureteral dilation and collecting system distortion indicating more significant anatomic deficiency. These grades are more likely to require surgical intervention. Grades I-II have resolution rates exceeding 80%. The system is image-based (VCUG), not pressure-based, symptom-based, or ultrasound-based.

stem: What surgical procedure is the gold standard for correcting high-grade VUR?

choices.feedback: Augmentation increases bladder capacity/compliance and is used for neurogenic bladder, not primary VUR correction.
Stents do not correct VUR. They are used temporarily for obstruction, not reflux.

Correct! Open ureteral reimplantation creates a long submucosal tunnel → anti-reflux mechanism (tunnel length:ureteral diameter ratio ≥5:1). Cohen technique: ureter crosses the trigone and enters contralateral bladder wall → long tunnel. Success rate >95-98%. Alternatives: extravesical (Lich-Gregoir — avoids opening bladder), endoscopic injection (Deflux — bulking agent injected at UO, ~70-80% success, less invasive but lower cure rate).
Pyeloplasty corrects UPJ obstruction, not VUR. Different anatomic site and pathology.

explanation: Cross-trigonal ureteral reimplantation (Cohen technique) is the gold standard open surgical procedure for correcting VUR, with success rates exceeding 95%. The ureter is tunneled across the trigone beneath the bladder mucosa, creating a longer submucosal tunnel that functions as a competent anti-reflux valve. Alternative techniques include Politano-Leadbetter (intravesical) and Lich-Gregoir (extravesical). Bladder augmentation is for neurogenic bladder with low compliance. Ureteral stenting does not correct VUR. UPJ pyeloplasty treats ureteropelvic junction obstruction, not VUR.

stem: What is the main concern with untreated high-grade VUR and recurrent febrile UTIs?

choices.feedback: While bladder dysfunction can coexist with VUR, the primary concern is renal scarring from refluxing infected urine.
VUR does not increase bladder cancer risk. The concern is renal.
VUR does not cause urethral stricture. Strictures result from instrumentation, infection, or trauma.

choices.text: Reflux nephropathy leading to renal scarring, hypertension, and CKD
choices.feedback: Correct! Reflux nephropathy: infected urine refluxing to the kidneys → pyelonephritis → renal scarring (especially in young children <5). Consequences: hypertension (renin-mediated from scarred kidneys), proteinuria, chronic kidney disease, potentially ESRD. DMSA scan detects scarring. Prevention: antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent febrile UTIs, early surgical correction for high-grade VUR or breakthrough infections. Scarring is irreversible once established.

explanation: The main concern with untreated high-grade VUR and recurrent febrile UTIs is reflux nephropathy — infected urine refluxing into the renal parenchyma causes acute pyelonephritis, leading to renal scarring. Progressive scarring results in hypertension, proteinuria, and chronic kidney disease (CKD). This is particularly concerning in young children whose kidneys are still developing. VUR does not cause overactive bladder (separate entity), bladder cancer, or urethral stricture.

stem: When is endoscopic injection (Deflux) preferred over open ureteral reimplantation for VUR?

choices.feedback: Deflux is used in primary VUR. Neurogenic bladder with VUR may require a different approach (address the underlying bladder dysfunction first).

choices.text: Only for adults, never for children
choices.feedback: Deflux is used for pediatric VUR and is most commonly used in children.

choices.text: Low-to-moderate grade VUR where a minimally invasive approach is desired
choices.feedback: Correct! Deflux (dextranomer/hyaluronic acid copolymer): injected submucosally at the ureteral orifice → creates a mound → anti-reflux mechanism. Advantages: outpatient, no incision, short procedure. Best results: Grades I-III (75-90% success with single injection). Grades IV-V: lower success (50-70%). Considered when: families prefer minimally invasive, lower-grade bilateral VUR, failed medical management. Open reimplant remains gold standard for high-grade or Deflux failures.

choices.text: For all grades of VUR as first-line surgical treatment
choices.feedback: Endoscopic injection has lower success rates (~70-80% vs >95%) and is not universally first-line.

explanation: Endoscopic injection (Deflux/STING or HIT procedure) is preferred over open reimplantation for low-to-moderate grade VUR (I-III) when a minimally invasive approach is desired by the family, as an outpatient procedure with lower morbidity than open surgery. It is not limited to neurogenic bladder patients. It is used in children (the primary VUR population), not exclusively adults. It is not first-line for all grades — success rates decline significantly for Grades IV-V, where open reimplantation remains superior.

stem: A 65-year-old man with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents with painless gross hematuria. Cystoscopy reveals a papillary lesion on the lateral bladder wall. Biopsy shows high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma confined to the lamina propria. What is the stage and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: T2 involves muscularis propria invasion. This lesion is T1 (lamina propria only), which is managed initially with TURBT + BCG.

choices.text: Stage T3; neoadjuvant chemotherapy then cystectomy
choices.feedback: T3 extends through the bladder wall into perivesical tissue. This lesion is confined to lamina propria (T1).

choices.text: Stage T1 (lamina propria invasion); transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) followed by intravesical BCG
choices.feedback: T1 high-grade urothelial carcinoma invades lamina propria but NOT muscularis propria. Treatment: TURBT for resection + restaging TURBT at 2-6 weeks (high risk of residual disease) + intravesical BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) immunotherapy. BCG is superior to intravesical chemotherapy for high-grade non-muscle-invasive disease. BCG activates immune response against residual tumor cells.

choices.text: Stage Ta; TURBT alone with surveillance
choices.feedback: Ta is confined to the mucosa (no lamina propria invasion). This biopsy shows lamina propria involvement = T1.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: What is the most significant modifiable risk factor for urothelial carcinoma of the bladder?

choices.feedback: Smoking is the #1 risk factor, responsible for ~50% of bladder cancers. Aromatic amines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are excreted renally and damage urothelium. Risk increases with intensity and duration. Other risk factors: occupational chemical exposure (aromatic amines in dye/rubber/leather industries), cyclophosphamide, chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection (squamous cell carcinoma).
Diet is not a major risk factor for bladder cancer.
Alcohol is not a significant risk factor for bladder cancer.
Chronic UTIs may increase squamous cell carcinoma risk but smoking is the dominant modifiable risk factor for urothelial carcinoma.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: A patient with BCG-unresponsive non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (T1 high-grade after adequate BCG) refuses radical cystectomy. What is an established treatment alternative?

choices.feedback: Pembrolizumab is used for BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC in patients who are ineligible for or decline cystectomy. It represents a bladder-sparing option. Response rate ~40%. Other options include intravesical therapies (gemcitabine/docetaxel, nadofaragene firadenovec gene therapy). Radical cystectomy remains the gold standard for BCG failures.
If the patient is BCG-unresponsive (failed adequate BCG with maintenance), further BCG is unlikely to help.
Radiation alone is not standard for NMIBC. Trimodal therapy (TURBT + chemoradiation) is a bladder-sparing option for muscle-invasive disease.
Oral methotrexate is not an approved treatment for BCG-unresponsive bladder cancer.

explanation: Correct: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1 immunotherapy).
auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: What percentage of newly diagnosed bladder cancers are non-muscle-invasive (stages Ta, T1, CIS)?

choices.feedback: The proportion is closer to 75% non-muscle-invasive.
Most bladder cancers are caught at a non-muscle-invasive stage. Only ~25% are muscle-invasive at diagnosis.

~75% of bladder cancers present as NMIBC (Ta ~50%, T1 ~20%, CIS ~5%). The remaining ~25% are muscle-invasive (≥T2) at diagnosis. NMIBC has high recurrence rates (50-70%) and progression risk to muscle-invasive disease (10-30% depending on grade). This is why surveillance cystoscopy is essential.
While most are NMIBC, ~25% present as muscle-invasive, so >90% is an overestimate.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: Which histologic type of bladder cancer is most strongly associated with chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection?

choices.feedback: Bladder adenocarcinoma is associated with urachal remnants and bladder exstrophy.

Chronic S. haematobium infection causes granulomatous inflammation → squamous metaplasia → squamous cell carcinoma. This is the predominant bladder cancer type in endemic regions (Egypt, sub-Saharan Africa). In Western countries, >90% of bladder cancers are urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma. Other SCC risk factors: chronic indwelling catheters, bladder stones.
Urothelial carcinoma is the most common type in Western countries but is not the type associated with schistosomiasis.
Small cell carcinoma of the bladder is rare and is not associated with schistosomiasis.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: A 62-year-old man presents with urinary frequency, nocturia, weak stream, and hesitancy. Digital rectal examination reveals a uniformly enlarged, smooth, nontender prostate. PSA is 2.1 ng/mL. What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment?

choices.feedback: BPH is not an infectious process. Antibiotics have no role unless concurrent UTI.

Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, silodosin) relax prostatic smooth muscle and bladder neck, providing rapid symptomatic relief (within days). They are first-line for moderate-severe LUTS/BPH. Side effects: orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation, intraoperative floppy iris syndrome. For large prostates (>30-40g), adding a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor (finasteride/dutasteride) reduces prostate volume over months.
Anticholinergics treat overactive bladder (urgency/frequency) but can worsen urinary retention in BPH.
5-alpha reductase inhibitors work slowly (3-6 months for full effect) and are most beneficial for larger prostates (>30-40g). Alpha-blockers provide faster symptom relief.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: A patient on tamsulosin for BPH develops acute urinary retention. After catheter decompression, what is the most appropriate definitive surgical treatment?

choices.feedback: Radical prostatectomy removes the entire prostate and is indicated for prostate CANCER, not BPH.

choices.text: Suprapubic catheter placement as permanent management
choices.feedback: Long-term catheterization is associated with infection and complications. Definitive surgical correction is preferred.
Orchiectomy eliminates testosterone and is used for metastatic prostate cancer, not BPH.

TURP is the gold standard surgical treatment for BPH when medical therapy fails or complications develop (retention, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, hematuria, renal insufficiency). It resects obstructing prostatic tissue transurethrally. Complications: retrograde ejaculation (~65%), bleeding, TUR syndrome (dilutional hyponatremia from glycine irrigation—use saline bipolar to avoid). Alternatives: HoLEP, UroLift, Rezūm.

explanation: Correct: Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP).
auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: What is the mechanism of action of finasteride in treating BPH?

choices.text: Blocks androgen receptors on prostatic cells
choices.feedback: Androgen receptor antagonists (e.g., flutamide, bicalutamide) are used for prostate cancer, not BPH.

choices.text: Relaxes prostatic smooth muscle via alpha receptor blockade
choices.feedback: This describes alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), not finasteride.

choices.text: Inhibits 5-alpha reductase, preventing conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT)
choices.feedback: 5-alpha reductase converts testosterone to DHT, which drives prostate growth. Finasteride (type II inhibitor) and dutasteride (types I and II) reduce prostate volume by 20-30% over 6-12 months. Side effects: decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, decreased ejaculate volume. Important: reduces PSA by ~50%, so double the measured PSA when screening for cancer.

choices.text: Inhibits GnRH release from the hypothalamus
choices.feedback: GnRH agonists/antagonists are used for prostate cancer (chemical castration), not BPH.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: Which validated questionnaire is used to assess the severity of lower urinary tract symptoms in BPH?

choices.feedback: ECOG measures overall functional status in oncology patients, not urinary symptoms.

The IPSS (also called AUA Symptom Score) is a 7-question validated tool scoring 0-35: mild (0-7), moderate (8-19), severe (20-35). Questions address incomplete emptying, frequency, intermittency, urgency, weak stream, straining, and nocturia. It guides treatment decisions and monitors response. Includes a quality-of-life question.
NIH-CPSI assesses chronic prostatitis/pelvic pain symptoms, not BPH.
Gleason score grades prostate cancer histopathology, not BPH symptoms.

explanation: Correct: International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS).
auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: Which zone of the prostate is most commonly involved in BPH?

choices.feedback: BPH arises from the transition zone surrounding the proximal urethra. As it enlarges, it compresses the urethra causing obstructive symptoms. In contrast, prostate cancer most commonly arises from the peripheral zone (detected by DRE). Central zone surrounds the ejaculatory ducts. This anatomic distinction explains why BPH causes early urinary symptoms while peripheral zone cancers may be asymptomatic until advanced.
The peripheral zone is where ~70% of prostate cancers arise, not BPH.
This is a non-glandular area and not the site of BPH development.
The central zone surrounds the ejaculatory ducts and is uncommonly the site of BPH or cancer.

explanation: Correct: Transition zone (periurethral).
auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: A 68-year-old man has a PSA of 8.5 ng/mL and an abnormal DRE with a firm nodule. TRUS-guided biopsy shows Gleason 3+4=7 adenocarcinoma in 3 of 12 cores. MRI shows no extracapsular extension. What is the clinical stage?

choices.feedback: MRI shows no extracapsular extension, ruling out T3.
T1c means the cancer was not detected on DRE. This patient has a palpable nodule.

Clinical staging: T1 = not palpable (found on biopsy for elevated PSA); T2a = palpable in half of one lobe; T2b = more than half of one lobe; T2c = both lobes; T3 = extracapsular extension; T4 = invades adjacent structures. A firm nodule on DRE = at least T2. MRI showing no extracapsular extension supports organ-confined disease. Gleason 3+4=7 is Grade Group 2 (intermediate risk).
T4 involves invasion of bladder, rectum, or pelvic wall, which is not present here.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: For localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer (Gleason 3+4=7), what are the standard treatment options with similar long-term oncologic outcomes?

choices.feedback: ADT alone is not curative for localized disease and is used as adjuvant therapy with radiation or for advanced/metastatic disease.

choices.text: Radical prostatectomy or definitive radiation therapy (external beam ± brachytherapy)
choices.feedback: For localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer, both radical prostatectomy and radiation therapy provide excellent long-term disease control with similar 10-15 year outcomes. Choice depends on patient preference, age, comorbidities, and side effect profile. Surgery: risk of urinary incontinence and ED. Radiation: risk of bowel symptoms, urinary irritation, and late ED. Active surveillance is primarily for low-risk (Gleason 3+3=6) disease.
Active surveillance is standard for very-low and low-risk disease (Gleason 3+3=6, Grade Group 1). Intermediate-risk disease generally warrants definitive treatment.
Chemotherapy is used for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, not localized disease.

auditNote: Batch 29: Verified accurate

stem: What is the most common site of metastatic spread in prostate cancer?

choices.feedback: Pulmonary metastases can occur but bone is the predominant site.

Prostate cancer characteristically produces osteoBLASTIC (bone-forming) metastases, in contrast to most other cancers which cause osteolytic lesions. Most common sites: axial skeleton (spine, pelvis, ribs). Detected by bone scan (technetium-99m). Elevated alkaline phosphatase suggests bony involvement. Other metastatic sites: lymph nodes, liver, lungs.
While liver metastases occur, bone is by far the most common site.
Brain metastases from prostate cancer are uncommon.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: What does a rising PSA after radical prostatectomy (biochemical recurrence) indicate?

choices.feedback: BPH cannot recur after complete prostate removal.
The prostate does not regenerate after complete removal. Any PSA rise indicates cancer recurrence.
While one value should be confirmed, persistent PSA rise after prostatectomy is not a lab error—it indicates recurrence.

choices.text: Residual or recurrent prostate cancer, since all PSA
choices.feedback: After radical prostatectomy, PSA should be undetectable (<0.2 ng/mL). Rising PSA = biochemical recurrence, indicating residual local disease or micrometastatic disease. Defined as PSA ≥0.2 ng/mL on two consecutive measurements. Rapid PSA doubling time (<3 months) suggests systemic disease; slower rise suggests local recurrence amenable to salvage radiation. PSMA-PET scan localizes recurrence.

explanation: Key detail: producing tissue should have been removed.
auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: Which Gleason Grade Group is associated with the best prognosis?

choices.feedback: GG3 is intermediate-unfavorable risk with worse prognosis than GG1 or GG2.
GG5 represents the most aggressive prostate cancer histology.

ISUP Grade Groups simplify Gleason scoring: GG1 (3+3=6), GG2 (3+4=7), GG3 (4+3=7), GG4 (4+4=8, 3+5=8, 5+3=8), GG5 (4+5=9, 5+4=9, 5+5=10). GG1 has excellent prognosis with near-zero cancer-specific mortality at 10 years. Most GG1 cancers are managed with active surveillance. The primary/secondary Gleason patterns matter: 3+4 has better prognosis than 4+3 (more aggressive primary pattern).

choices.text: All Gleason scores have similar prognosis
choices.feedback: Gleason/Grade Group is one of the strongest prognostic factors in prostate cancer.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a painless, firm testicular mass that does not transilluminate. Ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular lesion. What is the initial management?

choices.text: MRI of the testes before any intervention
choices.feedback: Ultrasound is the primary imaging. MRI may help in equivocal cases but should not delay orchiectomy for a suspicious solid mass.
Transscrotal biopsy/orchiectomy is CONTRAINDICATED—it can seed tumor into scrotal lymphatics, changing the metastatic pattern.
A solid intratesticular mass in a young man is cancer until proven otherwise. Antibiotics are appropriate for suspected epididymitis/orchitis, not solid masses.

Radical inguinal orchiectomy (NOT transscrotal biopsy) is the diagnostic and initial therapeutic procedure for suspected testicular cancer. The inguinal approach maintains the spermatic cord for pathologic evaluation and avoids violating scrotal planes (prevents local recurrence). Pre-operative serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) and chest/abdomen/pelvis CT are essential for staging.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: Which tumor marker pattern (elevated AFP, normal beta-hCG) is most consistent with which testicular germ cell tumor type?

choices.feedback: Pure seminomas may mildly elevate beta-hCG (~10% of cases) but NEVER elevate AFP.

Tumor markers by type: AFP elevated → yolk sac tumor (also mixed GCTs); beta-hCG elevated → choriocarcinoma (also some seminomas); LDH → tumor burden marker. Pure SEMINOMAS NEVER elevate AFP—if AFP is elevated, a nonseminomatous component is present regardless of histology. This distinction is critical because treatment protocols differ for seminoma vs. NSGCT.
Choriocarcinoma characteristically elevates beta-hCG dramatically, not AFP.
Mature teratomas typically do not significantly elevate AFP or beta-hCG.

explanation: Correct: Yolk sac tumor (endodermal sinus tumor).
auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: What is the most significant risk factor for testicular cancer?

choices.feedback: Trauma does not cause testicular cancer, though it may prompt examination that discovers an existing mass.
STIs cause epididymitis/orchitis but are not risk factors for testicular cancer.

Cryptorchidism increases testicular cancer risk 4-8x, even after orchiopexy. Risk applies to BOTH testes (not just the undescended one), suggesting a shared underlying abnormality. Orchiopexy before puberty may reduce risk and allows easier surveillance. Other risk factors: prior testicular cancer, family history, testicular dysgenesis syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome.
Vasectomy has not been shown to increase testicular cancer risk.

explanation: Correct: Cryptorchidism (undescended testis).
auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: Stage I seminoma after radical orchiectomy has an excellent prognosis. What are the management options?

choices.feedback: BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) is reserved for advanced (stage II-III) or relapsed disease. It is overtreatment for stage I seminoma.

choices.text: Surveillance, single-agent carboplatin, or adjuvant radiation—all with >95% cure rate
choices.feedback: Stage I seminoma cure rate is >99%. Options: (1) active surveillance (preferred for compliant patients—relapse rate ~15-20%, salvageable), (2) single-dose carboplatin (reduces relapse to ~5%), (3) adjuvant radiation to para-aortic nodes (largely replaced by carboplatin). Surveillance is increasingly favored to avoid treatment toxicity given the excellent salvage rates.
RPLND is primarily used for nonseminomatous tumors, not stage I seminoma.

choices.text: No further treatment or follow-up needed
choices.feedback: Even with excellent prognosis, surveillance with CT/markers is essential to detect the ~15-20% who relapse.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: What is the peak age incidence for testicular germ cell tumors?

choices.feedback: This age range is more typical for BPH and prostate cancer, not testicular cancer.
Testicular cancer is uncommon in elderly men. Testicular lymphoma is the most common testicular malignancy in men >60.

Testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in men aged 15-35. Seminomas peak slightly later (30-40 years) than NSGCTs (20-30 years). It is one of the most curable cancers when detected early. Monthly testicular self-examination is recommended for high-risk individuals. Incidence has been increasing over recent decades in Western countries.
While yolk sac tumors occur in young children, the overall peak incidence of testicular GCTs is in young adults.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the ED with acute onset severe left scrotal pain, nausea, and vomiting. The left testis is high-riding with a horizontal lie and an absent cremasteric reflex. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Ice, elevation, and analgesics with follow-up in 24 hours
choices.feedback: Delay risks permanent testicular loss. Torsion requires emergent surgical intervention.
Epididymitis is more common in sexually active adults. In adolescents with acute scrotum, torsion must be excluded first.

choices.text: Emergent surgical exploration—do NOT delay for imaging
choices.feedback: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency. The classic findings (acute scrotal pain, high-riding testis, horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex) have high clinical specificity. Testicular salvage rate: >90% if detorsion within 6 hours, ~50% at 12 hours, <10% after 24 hours. Do NOT delay surgery for ultrasound when clinical suspicion is high. Manual detorsion can be attempted ('open the book' = lateral rotation) while awaiting OR.
Ultrasound is useful when the diagnosis is uncertain, but should NOT delay surgical exploration when clinical findings strongly suggest torsion.

stem: During surgical exploration for testicular torsion, the affected testis appears viable after detorsion. What additional procedure should be performed?

choices.feedback: Orchiectomy is performed only if the testis is non-viable (necrotic) after detorsion. A viable testis is preserved.

After detorsion, the affected testis is fixed to the dartos fascia (orchiopexy) to prevent recurrence. The CONTRALATERAL testis must also be fixed because the bell-clapper deformity (inadequate fixation of tunica vaginalis) that predisposes to torsion is bilateral in most cases. Failure to fix the contralateral testis risks future torsion.

choices.text: No fixation needed if the testis reperfuses
choices.feedback: Without orchiopexy, torsion will likely recur. Fixation is always performed.

choices.text: Fixation of only the affected testis
choices.feedback: Both testes must be fixed. The bell-clapper deformity is typically bilateral.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with acute scrotal pain and a blue dot visible through the scrotal skin on the upper pole of the testis. Cremasteric reflex is intact and the testis is in normal position. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: The 'blue dot sign' on the upper pole of the testis is pathognomonic for torsion of the appendix testis (a Müllerian duct remnant). Key distinguishing features from testicular torsion: intact cremasteric reflex, testis in normal position, tenderness localized to the upper pole. Treatment is conservative: NSAIDs, scrotal support, activity restriction. Resolves in 5-7 days. No surgery needed.
Epididymitis in prepubertal boys is uncommon and presents with posterior tenderness, not a blue dot.
Testicular torsion presents with a high-riding testis, horizontal lie, and absent cremasteric reflex—none present here.
Tumors present as painless masses, not acute pain with a blue dot sign.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: What anatomical abnormality predisposes to testicular torsion?

choices.feedback: Retractile testes move between scrotum and inguinal canal due to a brisk cremasteric reflex but are not at increased risk for torsion.
Patent processus vaginalis predisposes to inguinal hernia and hydrocele, not testicular torsion.
Hypospadias is a urethral meatus malposition that does not predispose to torsion.

The bell-clapper deformity occurs when the tunica vaginalis attaches high on the spermatic cord rather than the posterior testis, allowing the testis to swing freely within the tunica like a clapper in a bell. This permits the testis to rotate on the spermatic cord → torsion → vascular compromise. Present in ~12% of males and is bilateral in most cases.

explanation: Correct: Bell-clapper deformity (high attachment of tunica vaginalis).
auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: Which clinical finding is most useful to distinguish testicular torsion from epididymitis?

choices.feedback: While torsion is more common in adolescents and epididymitis in adults, age alone is insufficient to differentiate.

choices.text: Absent cremasteric reflex in torsion vs. present in epididymitis
choices.feedback: The cremasteric reflex (stroking the inner thigh → ipsilateral testicular elevation) is absent in ~99% of testicular torsion cases and present in epididymitis. Other distinguishing features: torsion has sudden onset without urinary symptoms, high-riding testis; epididymitis has gradual onset, posterior tenderness, possible urinary symptoms, positive Prehn sign (relief with testicular elevation—though unreliable).
Pyuria suggests urinary tract infection/epididymitis but its absence does not exclude epididymitis or confirm torsion.
Both conditions are typically unilateral. Bilaterality does not distinguish them.

auditNote: Batch 30: Verified accurate

stem: A 5-year-old child with lumbosacral myelomeningocele undergoes urodynamic studies showing a small-capacity, high-pressure bladder with detrusor overactivity and detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia. What is the most important initial management to preserve renal function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clean intermittent catheterization plus oral oxybutynin

explanation:
NEUROGENIC BLADDER — INITIAL MANAGEMENT IN SPINA BIFIDA

Correct Answer: A — Clean intermittent catheterization plus oral oxybutynin

Neurogenic bladder in myelomeningocele results from disrupted innervation to the detrusor and sphincter. The most dangerous pattern is a high-pressure bladder (detrusor overactivity with sphincter dyssynergia), which causes vesicoureteral reflux, hydronephrosis, and progressive renal damage. First-line management combines clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) every 3–4 hours for safe bladder emptying and anticholinergic medication (oxybutynin) to reduce detrusor overactivity and lower storage pressures. The goal is maintaining bladder pressures <40 cm H₂O (the threshold above which upper tract damage occurs). This regimen has dramatically improved renal preservation in spina bifida patients.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Indwelling catheters increase UTI risk, urethral erosion, and bladder stone formation — CIC is strongly preferred
• C: Bladder augmentation (enterocystoplasty) is reserved for patients failing CIC + anticholinergics, not first-line therapy
• D: Observation risks progressive silent renal damage from high bladder pressures — active management is mandatory

Ref: StatPearls NBK560617 | Verified 2025
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 60-year-old man with a 10-year history of diabetes mellitus presents with progressive difficulty initiating urination, sensation of incomplete emptying, and recurrent urinary tract infections. Post-void residual volume is 350 mL. Urodynamic studies reveal an acontractile detrusor with absent sensation. What type of neurogenic bladder does this represent?
explanation:
DIABETIC CYSTOPATHY — LOWER MOTOR NEURON BLADDER

Correct Answer: A — Lower motor neuron (areflexic/flaccid) bladder

Diabetic cystopathy is a peripheral neuropathy of the bladder caused by damage to the pelvic parasympathetic nerves (S2-S4) and afferent sensory fibers. It represents a lower motor neuron (LMN) bladder pattern characterized by decreased bladder sensation, impaired detrusor contractility (acontractile/hypotonic detrusor), increased bladder capacity, and elevated post-void residual volumes. The progression is insidious: diminished sensation leads to infrequent voiding, chronic overdistension, and eventual myogenic failure. Treatment involves timed voiding, clean intermittent catheterization, and bethanechol (cholinergic agonist) may provide modest benefit. UTIs result from urinary stasis.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Upper motor neuron bladder (suprasacral lesion, e.g., spinal cord injury above S2) causes detrusor overactivity with small capacity and urgency — the opposite pattern
• C: DSD occurs with suprasacral spinal cord lesions where the detrusor contracts against a simultaneously contracting sphincter — not seen in peripheral neuropathy
• D: BPH causes obstruction with intact detrusor contractility (high-pressure voiding) — this patient has an acontractile detrusor

stem: A 25-year-old man with T8 spinal cord injury presents with sudden onset of severe headache, hypertension (210/120 mmHg), bradycardia, flushing above the injury level, and piloerection. His bladder is distended. What is the most important immediate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Catheterize the bladder immediately to relieve the stimulus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtain a CT scan of the head to rule out intracranial hemorrhage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give sublingual nifedipine and monitor blood pressure

explanation:
AUTONOMIC DYSREFLEXIA — EMERGENCY IN NEUROGENIC BLADDER

Correct Answer: A — Catheterize the bladder immediately to relieve the stimulus

Autonomic dysreflexia (AD) is a medical emergency in patients with spinal cord injury at or above T6. Noxious stimuli below the injury level (most commonly bladder distension from blocked catheter or urinary retention) trigger massive uninhibited sympathetic discharge, causing severe hypertension, reflex bradycardia, headache, flushing/sweating above the lesion, and pallor/piloerection below. The most critical step is identifying and removing the triggering stimulus — the bladder is the most common source (70–85% of cases). Sit the patient upright (postural BP reduction), loosen restrictive clothing, and catheterize immediately. If symptoms persist after bladder drainage, evaluate for fecal impaction. Pharmacologic BP management (nifedipine, nitropaste) is secondary to stimulus removal.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Antihypertensives are secondary — the priority is removing the stimulus, which will resolve the hypertension
• C: CT head delays the life-saving intervention; this presentation is classic AD, not stroke
• D: Sublingual nifedipine may be used as adjunct but can cause precipitous hypotension — stimulus removal is the priority

stem: A newborn male is found to have a palpable left testis in the inguinal canal and a nonpalpable right testis. At what age should surgical intervention (orchiopexy) be performed if the testes do not descend spontaneously?

choices.label: C
choices.text: After puberty to allow maximal hormonal stimulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: At age 5 years, before school entry

explanation:
CRYPTORCHIDISM — TIMING OF ORCHIOPEXY

Correct Answer: A — 6–12 months of age

Cryptorchidism (undescended testes) affects 3–5% of full-term males. Spontaneous descent occurs in approximately 70% by 3 months (driven by the postnatal testosterone surge). AUA and AAP guidelines recommend referral by 6 months and orchiopexy by 12 months (certainly by 18 months) if spontaneous descent has not occurred. Early surgery preserves fertility potential (germ cells begin degenerating by 12–18 months in undescended testes), reduces malignancy risk (cryptorchidism carries 4–8× increased risk of testicular cancer, partially mitigated by early orchiopexy), and allows easier testicular examination for cancer surveillance. For nonpalpable testes, diagnostic laparoscopy is indicated to locate intra-abdominal testes or confirm absence.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Surgery at birth is premature — spontaneous descent commonly occurs in the first 3–6 months
• C: Waiting until puberty allows irreversible germ cell damage and missed window for fertility preservation
• D: Age 5 is too late — significant testicular damage occurs from prolonged undescent after 12–18 months

stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a painless scrotal swelling that transilluminates and increases in size during crying or straining. The testis is palpable and normal within the swelling. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
COMMUNICATING HYDROCELE — PEDIATRIC UROLOGY

Correct Answer: A — Communicating hydrocele

A communicating hydrocele results from a patent processus vaginalis that allows peritoneal fluid to flow into the tunica vaginalis around the testis. Key features include: painless scrotal swelling, positive transillumination (distinguishing fluid from solid mass), size changes with position or straining (fluid moves between peritoneum and scrotum via the patent processus), and a normal palpable testis within the fluid collection. Most congenital hydroceles resolve spontaneously by 12–24 months as the processus vaginalis closes. Surgical repair (ligation of the processus vaginalis) is indicated if the hydrocele persists beyond 12–24 months or if an associated inguinal hernia is present.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Inguinal hernia does not transilluminate (bowel contents are opaque), and bowel may produce visible peristalsis and be reducible
• C: Testicular tumors are solid masses that do not transilluminate and present as painless, firm testicular enlargement
• D: Varicocele feels like a "bag of worms," does not transilluminate, and is uncommon before puberty

stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought for evaluation of a urethral meatus located on the ventral surface of the penile shaft. The foreskin has a dorsal hooded appearance. Which statement about the management of this condition is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Circumcision should be deferred because the foreskin may be needed for surgical repair

choices.label: B
choices.text: Circumcision should be performed immediately to visualize the meatus better

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical repair should be delayed until after puberty

choices.label: D
choices.text: No treatment is needed as this is a normal variant

explanation:
HYPOSPADIAS — MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES

Correct Answer: A — Circumcision should be deferred because the foreskin may be needed for surgical repair

Hypospadias is a congenital anomaly where the urethral meatus opens on the ventral surface of the penis, occurring in approximately 1 in 200–300 male births. The dorsal hooded prepuce is a classic associated finding. A critical management principle is that circumcision must be avoided in any infant with hypospadias because the preputial tissue serves as a potential graft or flap source during surgical repair (urethroplasty). Repair is typically performed between 6–18 months of age. The goals of surgery are to create a slit-like meatus at the tip of the glans, straighten any chordee (ventral curvature), and achieve cosmetically normal appearance. More proximal defects may require staged repair.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Circumcision destroys tissue that may be essential for repair — this is a critical contraindication
• C: Repair at 6–18 months optimizes outcomes; delaying until puberty increases psychological impact and technical difficulty
• D: Hypospadias can cause functional problems (abnormal urinary stream, difficulties with intercourse) and requires surgical correction

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with painless gross hematuria, left flank pain, and a palpable abdominal mass. CT scan reveals a 9 cm heterogeneous enhancing mass in the left kidney with tumor thrombus extending into the left renal vein. What is the most appropriate treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Radical nephrectomy with renal vein thrombectomy

explanation:
RENAL CELL CARCINOMA — SURGICAL MANAGEMENT

Correct Answer: A — Radical nephrectomy with renal vein thrombectomy

The classic triad of RCC (hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass) is present in only 6–10% of cases and typically indicates advanced disease. This patient has a large (>7 cm, T2+) renal mass with venous tumor thrombus (T3a — renal vein invasion), requiring radical nephrectomy (removal of kidney, Gerota's fascia, and ipsilateral adrenal gland if involved) with venous thrombectomy. RCC has a propensity for venous invasion, extending from the renal vein into the IVC and occasionally to the right atrium. Tumor thrombus extent dictates surgical complexity. Clear cell RCC is the most common histologic subtype (70–80%).

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Partial nephrectomy is indicated for tumors ≤7 cm (T1) or when nephron-sparing is imperative — not appropriate for large tumors with venous invasion
• C: RCC is classically resistant to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy — targeted therapies (TKIs, checkpoint inhibitors) are used for metastatic disease
• D: RCC is considered a radioresistant tumor — radiation has no role in primary treatment

stem: A 62-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma is being evaluated for systemic therapy after nephrectomy. Which class of medications has become the standard first-line treatment for advanced RCC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immune checkpoint inhibitor combination (nivolumab + ipilimumab) or TKI plus checkpoint inhibitor

explanation:
METASTATIC RCC — SYSTEMIC THERAPY

Correct Answer: A — Immune checkpoint inhibitor combination or TKI plus checkpoint inhibitor

First-line treatment for metastatic clear cell RCC has evolved dramatically. Current standard-of-care regimens include checkpoint inhibitor combinations: nivolumab (anti-PD-1) + ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) for intermediate/poor-risk disease, or TKI + checkpoint inhibitor combinations (pembrolizumab + axitinib; nivolumab + cabozantinib) for all risk categories. These regimens have demonstrated superior overall survival compared to TKI monotherapy. RCC is an immunogenic tumor, which explains the efficacy of immunotherapy. For favorable-risk patients, TKI monotherapy (sunitinib, pazopanib) remains an option. The mTOR inhibitors (temsirolimus, everolimus) are used in later lines.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: RCC is inherently resistant to cytotoxic chemotherapy due to expression of multidrug resistance (MDR) genes
• C: Hormonal therapy was historically tried but has no significant efficacy in RCC
• D: IFN-alpha was a historical standard but has been supplanted by far more effective targeted and immunotherapy regimens

stem: A 50-year-old man is found to have bilateral renal masses, retinal hemangioblastomas, and a pheochromocytoma. Genetic testing confirms a germline mutation. Which hereditary syndrome is associated with this presentation?
explanation:
VON HIPPEL-LINDAU SYNDROME — HEREDITARY RCC

Correct Answer: A — Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome

Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the VHL tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 3p25. VHL protein normally targets hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) for degradation; loss leads to constitutive HIF activation and overproduction of VEGF, driving tumor angiogenesis. Key manifestations include clear cell RCC (often bilateral and multifocal), retinal and CNS hemangioblastomas (cerebellar most common), pheochromocytomas, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, and endolymphatic sac tumors. RCC is the leading cause of death in VHL patients. Management emphasizes nephron-sparing surgery when possible due to bilateral disease and lifelong surveillance protocols.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Tuberous sclerosis causes renal angiomyolipomas (not clear cell RCC), cortical tubers, and cardiac rhabdomyomas
• C: Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome (FLCN mutation) causes chromophobe RCC, pulmonary cysts, and fibrofolliculomas — not hemangioblastomas
• D: Hereditary papillary RCC involves MET proto-oncogene mutations causing type 1 papillary RCC — not associated with hemangioblastomas or pheochromocytomas

stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a painless, firm testicular mass that does not transilluminate. Scrotal ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular mass. Serum AFP is markedly elevated and beta-hCG is mildly elevated. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scrotal exploration with intraoperative frozen section

explanation:
TESTICULAR CANCER — RADICAL INGUINAL ORCHIECTOMY

Correct Answer: A — Radical inguinal orchiectomy

Any solid intratesticular mass in a young man is testicular cancer until proven otherwise. Radical inguinal orchiectomy (through an inguinal incision with early ligation of the spermatic cord at the internal ring) is both diagnostic and the initial therapeutic step. The inguinal approach prevents violation of scrotal lymphatic drainage, which would alter the pattern of potential metastatic spread. Elevated AFP indicates a nonseminomatous component (yolk sac tumor elements), as pure seminomas NEVER produce AFP. Beta-hCG can be elevated in both seminomas and nonseminomatous tumors. Testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in males aged 15–35. Staging CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis follows orchiectomy.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Transscrotal biopsy is CONTRAINDICATED — it risks local seeding and alters lymphatic drainage patterns
• C: Retroperitoneal biopsy is not appropriate before orchiectomy and histologic diagnosis
• D: Scrotal exploration violates the scrotal compartment — the inguinal approach is mandatory

stem: Pathology from a radical orchiectomy in a 25-year-old reveals a mixed germ cell tumor containing embryonal carcinoma and yolk sac elements. Post-orchiectomy AFP normalizes but CT shows a 3 cm retroperitoneal lymph node. After staging, what is the most appropriate treatment?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Observation with serial imaging and tumor markers

explanation:
NONSEMINOMATOUS GCT — CHEMOTHERAPY FOR NODAL DISEASE

Correct Answer: A — BEP chemotherapy (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin)

Nonseminomatous germ cell tumors (NSGCT) with retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (stage II) are treated with cisplatin-based chemotherapy, most commonly BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) for 3–4 cycles depending on risk stratification. Testicular cancer is one of the most chemosensitive solid tumors, with cure rates exceeding 95% for early-stage and 70–80% for advanced disease. After chemotherapy, if residual retroperitoneal masses persist, post-chemotherapy retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (PC-RPLND) is performed to resect residual teratoma or viable tumor. Tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) are followed serially.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Radiation is the primary adjuvant for stage II seminoma, not nonseminomatous GCTs (which are relatively radioresistant)
• C: Surveillance is an option for clinical stage I NSGCT but not for stage II with documented lymph node metastasis
• D: Primary RPLND may be appropriate for low-volume stage IIA NSGCT in selected cases, but with a 3 cm node, chemotherapy is preferred

stem: A 32-year-old man undergoes radical orchiectomy and pathology reveals pure seminoma confined to the testis (stage I). Post-orchiectomy tumor markers are normal and CT imaging shows no lymphadenopathy. What is the preferred management approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Active surveillance with serial imaging and tumor markers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adjuvant radiation to para-aortic lymph nodes

explanation:
STAGE I SEMINOMA — ACTIVE SURVEILLANCE

Correct Answer: A — Active surveillance with serial imaging and tumor markers

Active surveillance has become the preferred management for stage I seminoma after orchiectomy, as the recurrence rate is only 15–20% and nearly all recurrences are curable with salvage therapy. Surveillance protocols include periodic CT imaging, tumor markers (beta-hCG, LDH), and physical examination over 5–10 years. This approach avoids the toxicity of adjuvant therapy in the 80–85% of patients who are already cured by orchiectomy alone. When adjuvant therapy is chosen (for patients with risk factors or inability to comply with surveillance), options include single-agent carboplatin (1–2 cycles) or para-aortic radiation — though the trend is moving away from radiation to minimize long-term toxicity.

• B: BEP is used for advanced disease — single-agent carboplatin is the adjuvant chemo option for stage I seminoma if needed

stem: Prenatal ultrasound at 28 weeks reveals unilateral hydronephrosis in a male fetus with an anteroposterior renal pelvic diameter of 15 mm. Postnatal ultrasound at 1 week confirms persistent hydronephrosis with a dilated renal pelvis but no ureteral dilation or bladder abnormality. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
UPJ OBSTRUCTION — PRENATAL HYDRONEPHROSIS

Correct Answer: A — Ureteropelvic junction obstruction

Ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) obstruction is the most common cause of significant prenatal hydronephrosis, accounting for approximately 35% of cases. It results from intrinsic narrowing at the junction of the renal pelvis and proximal ureter (most commonly from an aperistaltic segment) or extrinsic compression (crossing lower pole vessel). The hallmark finding is a dilated renal pelvis WITHOUT ureteral dilation — this distinguishes UPJ obstruction from ureterovesical junction obstruction (which shows ureteral and pelvic dilation) and VUR (which shows variable hydroureteronephrosis). Postnatal evaluation includes renal ultrasound (timing depends on severity) and diuretic renogram (MAG3 with furosemide) to assess differential function and drainage.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: PUV causes bilateral hydronephrosis WITH dilated bladder and posterior urethra — occurs only in males
• C: VUR causes hydroureteronephrosis (dilated ureter + pelvis) and is diagnosed by VCUG, not ultrasound alone
• D: UVJ obstruction causes hydroureter AND hydronephrosis — the ureter would be dilated proximal to the junction

stem: A 6-month-old infant with UPJ obstruction undergoes diuretic renogram showing the affected kidney contributes 35% of total renal function with a prolonged T1/2 washout of 40 minutes. The contralateral kidney is normal. What is the most appropriate management?
explanation:
UPJ OBSTRUCTION — SURGICAL INTERVENTION

Correct Answer: A — Dismembered pyeloplasty (Anderson-Hynes)

Dismembered pyeloplasty is the gold standard surgical repair for UPJ obstruction, with success rates >95%. Indications for surgery include differential renal function <40% (this patient has 35%), decreasing function on serial renography, worsening hydronephrosis, symptomatic episodes (pain, UTI, stones), or T1/2 >20 minutes on diuretic renogram. The Anderson-Hynes technique involves excising the obstructed UPJ segment, spatulating the ureter, reducing the redundant pelvis, and creating a tension-free, widely patent anastomosis. If a crossing vessel is identified, the ureter is transposed anterior to it. Robotic-assisted and laparoscopic approaches are increasingly used.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Observation is appropriate for mild hydronephrosis with preserved function (>40%) and no symptoms — this patient meets surgical criteria
• C: Ureteral stenting does not definitively correct UPJ obstruction and is used only as a temporizing measure
• D: Nephrectomy is reserved for non-functioning kidneys (<10% function) — this kidney contributes 35%

stem: A 14-year-old adolescent presents with intermittent left flank pain that worsens after drinking large volumes of fluid. Ultrasound reveals left hydronephrosis. CT angiography identifies a crossing lower pole renal artery at the UPJ. What is the mechanism of obstruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extrinsic compression of the UPJ by the crossing vessel

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intrinsic aperistaltic segment of the ureter

explanation:
UPJ OBSTRUCTION — CROSSING VESSEL ETIOLOGY

Correct Answer: A — Extrinsic compression of the UPJ by the crossing vessel

Crossing lower pole renal vessels are found in approximately 30–50% of UPJ obstructions and are more common in older children and adults presenting with intermittent symptoms. The vessel compresses the UPJ extrinsically, particularly when urine flow increases (hence the classic Dietl crisis — episodic flank pain triggered by diuresis from large fluid intake). This is in contrast to the more common intrinsic cause in neonates (aperistaltic ureteral segment with abnormal collagen-to-muscle ratio). Surgical management must address the crossing vessel — the ureter is transposed anterior to the vessel during pyeloplasty to prevent recurrent obstruction.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Intrinsic aperistaltic segment is the most common cause in neonates but this adolescent has a documented crossing vessel

stem: A 55-year-old multiparous woman reports involuntary urine loss when coughing, sneezing, or exercising. She denies urgency or frequency. Physical examination reveals urethral hypermobility with a positive cough stress test. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
explanation:
STRESS URINARY INCONTINENCE — FIRST-LINE TREATMENT

Correct Answer: A — Pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises)

Stress urinary incontinence (SUI) is characterized by involuntary urine leakage with increased abdominal pressure (coughing, sneezing, exercise, laughing) in the absence of detrusor contraction. It is the most common type of incontinence in women, caused by urethral hypermobility (weakened pelvic floor support) and/or intrinsic sphincter deficiency. First-line treatment for all types of urinary incontinence is behavioral therapy, with pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT/Kegel exercises) being the cornerstone for SUI. Supervised PFMT with biofeedback achieves 50–80% improvement. Additional conservative measures include weight loss, fluid management, and pessary devices. Surgical options (midurethral sling — the gold standard) are reserved for patients failing conservative therapy.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Oxybutynin (anticholinergic) treats urgency/overactive bladder — it has no role in pure stress incontinence
• C: Midurethral sling is the most effective surgical treatment but is second-line after behavioral therapy
• D: Sacral neuromodulation is indicated for refractory urgency incontinence/overactive bladder, not stress incontinence

stem: A 70-year-old woman reports frequent sudden, intense urges to urinate followed by involuntary leakage before reaching the bathroom. She voids 12 times per day and 3 times at night. Urodynamic studies show involuntary detrusor contractions during filling. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment?
explanation:
URGENCY INCONTINENCE / OVERACTIVE BLADDER — PHARMACOTHERAPY

Correct Answer: A — Oral antimuscarinics or beta-3 agonist

Urgency urinary incontinence (UUI) results from involuntary detrusor contractions (detrusor overactivity) during bladder filling. First-line pharmacotherapy includes antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin) which block M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle, or the beta-3 adrenergic agonist mirabegron which relaxes the detrusor during filling. Antimuscarinics should be used cautiously in elderly patients due to anticholinergic side effects (dry mouth, constipation, cognitive impairment, urinary retention). Mirabegron has a favorable side effect profile in older adults. Behavioral therapy (bladder training, timed voiding) should be combined with pharmacotherapy. Refractory cases may benefit from onabotulinumtoxinA injection, sacral neuromodulation, or percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation.

• B: Alpha-blockers treat bladder outlet obstruction (BPH) in men — they do not address detrusor overactivity

stem: A 78-year-old man in a nursing home with dementia has continuous urinary dribbling. His bladder is distended on examination with a post-void residual of 600 mL. His medications include oxybutynin, diphenhydramine, and an antipsychotic. What is the most likely type of incontinence?

OVERFLOW INCONTINENCE — URINARY RETENTION

Correct Answer: A — Overflow incontinence from urinary retention

Overflow incontinence results from chronic urinary retention when intravesical pressure exceeds urethral resistance, causing continuous dribbling from an overdistended bladder. In this patient, multiple anticholinergic medications (oxybutynin, diphenhydramine, and many antipsychotics) compound to impair detrusor contractility, contributing to urinary retention. The distended bladder with PVR of 600 mL confirms retention. In men, BPH is also a common contributing factor. Management includes catheterization for immediate decompression, medication review to eliminate unnecessary anticholinergics, and evaluation for outlet obstruction. This case illustrates the anticholinergic burden concept — critical in geriatric medicine.

stem: A 1-year-old female infant presents with her second febrile urinary tract infection. Renal ultrasound shows left hydronephrosis. Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) reveals grade III vesicoureteral reflux on the left. What is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A

explanation:
VESICOURETERAL REFLUX — INITIAL MANAGEMENT

Correct Answer: A — Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis with monitoring for spontaneous resolution

Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) is graded I–V based on VCUG findings (I = reflux into ureter only; V = massive reflux with ureteral tortuosity and calyceal blunting). Grades I–III have high rates of spontaneous resolution (80–90% for grade I-II, 50% for grade III) as the intravesical ureteral tunnel lengthens with growth. Initial management for low-to-moderate grade VUR (I–III) in young children includes continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or nitrofurantoin) to prevent recurrent UTIs and renal scarring, with serial VCUG or radionuclide cystography to monitor for resolution. DMSA renal scan evaluates for existing renal scarring.

• B: Surgical reimplantation is reserved for high-grade VUR (IV-V), breakthrough UTIs on prophylaxis, or failure to resolve
• C: Endoscopic injection is a less invasive alternative to surgery but is not first-line for grade III VUR in a young child with high spontaneous resolution potential

Open ureteral reimplantation is indicated for high-grade VUR (grade IV-V), breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis, progressive renal scarring, or failure of VUR to resolve with conservative management. The Cohen cross-trigonal technique is the most commonly performed reimplantation, achieving success rates >95%. The principle is creating a longer intravesical submucosal tunnel (5:1 tunnel-to-ureteral diameter ratio) that provides a flap-valve mechanism to prevent reflux. The Lich-Gregoir extravesical approach is an alternative with comparable results. Postoperative VCUG confirms resolution of reflux.

stem: A pediatric resident reviews the embryologic basis of primary vesicoureteral reflux. Which anatomic abnormality is the fundamental cause of primary VUR?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Short intravesical ureteral tunnel with a laterally displaced ureteral orifice

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absent ureteral smooth muscle at the ureterovesical junction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ectopic ureteral insertion into the urethra

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duplication of the ureteral bud with both ureters refluxing

explanation:
PRIMARY VUR — EMBRYOLOGIC BASIS

Correct Answer: A — Short intravesical ureteral tunnel with a laterally displaced ureteral orifice

Primary VUR results from a congenital deficiency of the intravesical ureteral tunnel. Normally, the ureter traverses obliquely through the bladder wall for a distance of 4–5 times its diameter, creating a passive flap-valve mechanism that prevents reflux during bladder contraction. In primary VUR, the ureteral bud arises more cranially from the mesonephric duct during development, resulting in a laterally displaced, gaping ureteral orifice with a shortened intravesical tunnel. The more lateral the orifice position, the shorter the tunnel and the higher the grade of reflux. This is why VUR can spontaneously resolve — as the bladder grows, the tunnel lengthens, improving the valve mechanism.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: The ureteral muscle is present but the tunnel geometry is inadequate — muscle absence would be a more severe structural anomaly
• C: Ectopic ureters (inserting beyond the trigone into the urethra, vestibule, or vagina) cause continuous incontinence, not typical VUR
• D: While duplex ureters can be associated with VUR (usually the lower pole ureter refluxes per the Weigert-Meyer rule), this is not the fundamental cause of primary VUR

explanation:
DETRUSOR-SPHINCTER DYSSYNERGIA

Correct Answer: A — Detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia

Detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia (DSD) occurs in suprasacral spinal cord lesions (above S2-S4) where the coordinating pathway between the pontine micturition center and the sacral cord is disrupted. The detrusor contracts (upper motor neuron bladder) but the external urethral sphincter simultaneously contracts instead of relaxing, creating a functional obstruction. This produces high intravesical pressures, incomplete emptying (high PVR), and risk of vesicoureteral reflux, hydronephrosis, and renal damage. Management includes clean intermittent catheterization (CIC), anticholinergics to reduce detrusor overactivity, and potentially botulinum toxin injection into the detrusor or sphincter. Annual renal ultrasound and urodynamics monitoring are essential.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Detrusor underactivity (areflexic/LMN bladder) occurs with sacral/peripheral lesions — the detrusor fails to contract
• C: Stress incontinence is from pelvic floor weakness, not neurologic injury
• D: Overflow from atonic bladder occurs in lower motor neuron lesions (cauda equina), not suprasacral injuries

stem: A child with myelomeningocele (spina bifida) has a neurogenic bladder. What is the first-line management to prevent upper urinary tract deterioration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clean intermittent catheterization plus anticholinergic medication

explanation:
NEUROGENIC BLADDER IN SPINA BIFIDA — FIRST-LINE MANAGEMENT

Correct Answer: A — Clean intermittent catheterization plus anticholinergic medication

The goal in pediatric neurogenic bladder management is preserving renal function by maintaining low-pressure bladder storage. CIC (typically every 3–4 hours) ensures regular bladder emptying, while anticholinergics (oxybutynin) reduce detrusor overactivity and lower storage pressures. This combined approach is started early, even in neonates with spina bifida, based on urodynamic findings of high detrusor pressures (>40 cm H2O) or detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia. Regular surveillance with renal ultrasound, urodynamics, and VCUG monitors for hydronephrosis and reflux. Bladder augmentation (enterocystoplasty) is reserved for medically refractory cases where CIC + anticholinergics fail to achieve safe bladder pressures.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Chronic indwelling catheters increase UTI risk, bladder stone formation, and urethral erosion — not appropriate for long-term management
• C: Augmentation is a last resort after medical management fails
• D: Waiting for symptoms risks irreversible renal damage from chronically elevated bladder pressures

stem: A patient with diabetic autonomic neuropathy has progressive difficulty emptying the bladder, with increasing postvoid residuals exceeding 300 mL. Urodynamics show poor detrusor contractility. What type of neurogenic bladder is this?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mixed upper and lower motor neuron bladder

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sensory neurogenic bladder with normal detrusor function

explanation:
LOWER MOTOR NEURON BLADDER — DIABETIC NEUROPATHY

Correct Answer: B — Lower motor neuron (flaccid/areflexic) bladder

Diabetic autonomic neuropathy affects the peripheral parasympathetic nerves (pelvic splanchnic nerves S2–S4) that innervate the detrusor muscle, resulting in a lower motor neuron (LMN) bladder pattern. The detrusor becomes hypotonic/areflexic, unable to generate adequate contractile pressure for emptying. The bladder gradually distends, compliance increases, and large postvoid residuals accumulate, leading to overflow incontinence. Other causes of LMN bladder include cauda equina syndrome, sacral cord injury, pelvic surgery, and herniated discs affecting sacral roots. Management is CIC (cornerstone), avoidance of overdistension, and possibly bethanechol (cholinergic agonist, limited efficacy).

Why other answers are incorrect:
• A: UMN bladder (suprasacral lesions) shows detrusor overactivity/hyperreflexia, not poor contractility
• C: Mixed pattern occurs with lesions at multiple levels — this presentation is purely LMN
• D: Sensory neurogenic bladder (tabes dorsalis) has impaired sensation but initially preserved motor function — diabetic neuropathy affects both sensory and motor pathways

stem: A newborn boy has bilateral nonpalpable testes. Which initial evaluation is most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Karyotype and endocrine evaluation to rule out disorders of sex development

explanation:
BILATERAL NONPALPABLE TESTES — EVALUATION

Correct Answer: A — Karyotype and endocrine evaluation to rule out disorders of sex development

Bilateral nonpalpable testes in a newborn is a clinical emergency because it may represent a 46,XX disorder of sex development (DSD) — specifically congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) in a virilized female, which can be life-threatening due to salt-wasting crisis. Urgent evaluation includes karyotype, serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone (elevated in 21-hydroxylase deficiency CAH), electrolytes (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia), testosterone, LH/FSH, and AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone — elevated confirms testicular tissue). Ultrasound has poor sensitivity for intra-abdominal testes (~45%) and should not delay endocrine workup. If testes are confirmed present, orchidopexy is recommended by 6–12 months to preserve fertility potential and allow testicular examination.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Ultrasound has low sensitivity for localizing intra-abdominal testes and does not address the DSD concern
• C: Surgery is not the first step — endocrine evaluation is needed to establish the diagnosis and identify potentially life-threatening conditions
• D: Observation alone is dangerous — CAH can cause adrenal crisis within the first weeks of life

stem: A 2-year-old boy has a right undescended testis palpable in the inguinal canal that cannot be manipulated into the scrotum. What is the recommended management?
explanation:
CRYPTORCHIDISM — ORCHIDOPEXY

Correct Answer: A — Orchidopexy (surgical fixation into the scrotum)

Oorchidopexy is the definitive treatment for cryptorchidism (undescended testis) and is recommended by 6–12 months of age, with surgery no later than 18 months. After 6 months, spontaneous descent is unlikely. Early orchidopexy preserves fertility potential (germ cell degeneration begins by 12 months in undescended testes), reduces malignancy risk (cryptorchid testes have 4–8× increased risk of testicular cancer even after orchidopexy), and allows clinical examination for early cancer detection. For palpable inguinal testes, an inguinal orchidopexy is performed. At 2 years old, this child has already exceeded the ideal surgical window, making prompt orchidopexy essential.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Hormonal therapy (hCG, GnRH) has low success rates (<20%) and is not recommended as primary treatment by current AUA/EAU guidelines
• C: Observation after 6 months is inappropriate — spontaneous descent is unlikely, and delay worsens outcomes
• D: Orchiectomy is reserved for postpubertal patients with intra-abdominal testes or atrophic testes, not palpable inguinal testes in children

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with painless swelling of the left hemiscrotum that transilluminates brightly. The swelling does not reduce with position changes and has been slowly growing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
NON-COMMUNICATING HYDROCELE

Correct Answer: B — Non-communicating hydrocele

A non-communicating hydrocele is a collection of peritoneal fluid around the testis within the tunica vaginalis where the processus vaginalis has closed (no communication with the peritoneal cavity). It transilluminates brightly, does not change size with position or Valsalva, and is painless. In children over 2 years, non-communicating hydroceles that persist or enlarge warrant surgical repair (hydrocelectomy) because they don't resolve spontaneously like neonatal hydroceles. In contrast, communicating hydroceles (patent processus vaginalis) fluctuate in size throughout the day and are repaired like inguinal hernias because they share the same anatomic defect.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• A: Inguinal hernias typically reduce with position changes or manual pressure; they may not transilluminate unless containing bowel
• C: Testicular torsion presents with acute severe pain, not painless progressive swelling
• D: Varicoceles feel like a "bag of worms," are typically left-sided, increase with standing/Valsalva, and do not transilluminate

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with hematuria, flank pain, and a palpable abdominal mass. CT reveals a 9 cm enhancing renal mass with tumor thrombus extending into the renal vein. Which subtype accounts for approximately 70-80% of renal cell carcinomas?
explanation:
CLEAR CELL RCC — MOST COMMON SUBTYPE

Correct Answer: A — Clear cell renal cell carcinoma

Clear cell RCC accounts for 70–80% of renal cell carcinomas and is characterized by VHL gene inactivation (chromosome 3p deletion) in both sporadic and hereditary (von Hippel-Lindau disease) forms. VHL loss leads to constitutive HIF activation, upregulating VEGF and promoting angiogenesis — explaining why clear cell RCC is highly vascular. The classic triad of hematuria, flank pain, and palpable mass occurs in <10% of patients (most are now incidentally discovered on imaging). RCC is known as the "internist's tumor" due to paraneoplastic syndromes: erythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without metastases), hypertension (renin), and secondary amyloidosis. Treatment for localized disease is radical or partial nephrectomy.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Papillary RCC (10–15%) has two subtypes and is associated with MET mutations and trisomies 7/17
• C: Chromophobe RCC (5%) has the best prognosis of all RCC subtypes
• D: Collecting duct carcinoma (<1%) is rare and aggressive

stem: A patient with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma is being considered for systemic therapy. Which first-line treatment combination is currently standard of care?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immune checkpoint inhibitor combination (nivolumab + ipilimumab or pembrolizumab + axitinib)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cytotoxic chemotherapy with cisplatin and gemcitabine

explanation:
METASTATIC RCC — IMMUNOTHERAPY-BASED COMBINATIONS

Correct Answer: A — Immune checkpoint inhibitor combination

Metastatic clear cell RCC is treated with immunotherapy-based combinations as first-line therapy. Current NCCN-recommended regimens include: nivolumab (anti-PD-1) + ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) for intermediate/poor-risk disease, or pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) + axitinib (VEGF-TKI) for all risk categories. These combinations have demonstrated superior overall survival compared to single-agent TKIs in landmark trials (CheckMate 214, KEYNOTE-426). RCC is classically chemoresistant — traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy has response rates <10%. The treatment landscape has shifted from cytokines (IL-2, interferon) to targeted therapy (sunitinib, cabozantinib) to current immunotherapy combinations over the past two decades.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: RCC is notoriously chemoresistant; cisplatin-based regimens are ineffective
• C: Interferon-alpha was used historically but has been replaced by far more effective immunotherapy combinations
• D: RCC is considered radioresistant; radiation is used only for palliation (bone metastases, brain metastases) or stereotactic approaches

stem: A 35-year-old patient with bilateral renal masses, retinal hemangioblastomas, and cerebellar tumors is found to have a germline mutation. Which hereditary syndrome and associated gene mutation should be tested?
explanation:
VON HIPPEL-LINDAU SYNDROME — BILATERAL RCC

Correct Answer: A — Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome (VHL gene mutation)

VHL syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline VHL tumor suppressor gene mutations (chromosome 3p25). The VHL protein normally targets HIF-alpha for proteasomal degradation; when mutated, HIF accumulates and drives VEGF/PDGF/TGF expression leading to highly vascular tumors. VHL manifestations include: clear cell RCC (bilateral/multifocal in ~40% of patients), retinal hemangioblastomas (often earliest manifestation), cerebellar/spinal hemangioblastomas, pheochromocytomas, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and cysts, and endolymphatic sac tumors. RCC develops in ~70% of VHL patients by age 60. Management involves nephron-sparing surgery when possible to preserve renal function, with surveillance beginning at age 15.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Birt-Hogg-Dubé features chromophobe/hybrid oncocytic RCC, fibrofolliculomas, and spontaneous pneumothorax — not retinal/cerebellar tumors
• C: Hereditary papillary RCC (MET mutations) causes bilateral papillary (not clear cell) tumors without extrarenal manifestations
• D: Tuberous sclerosis causes renal angiomyolipomas (not RCC), cortical tubers, and cardiac rhabdomyomas

stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a painless, firm testicular mass discovered on self-examination. Ultrasound confirms a 3 cm solid intratesticular mass. Serum AFP is elevated, beta-hCG is mildly elevated, and LDH is normal. What is the most likely histologic diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nonseminomatous germ cell tumor (mixed with yolk sac component)

explanation:
NONSEMINOMATOUS GCT — TUMOR MARKER PATTERN

Correct Answer: A — Nonseminomatous germ cell tumor (mixed with yolk sac component)

Testicular germ cell tumors (GCTs) are divided into seminomas and nonseminomatous GCTs (NSGCTs: embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, teratoma, and mixed). Tumor markers are essential for classification: AFP is elevated ONLY in NSGCTs (yolk sac tumor is the primary AFP source), beta-hCG can be elevated in both (but markedly elevated in choriocarcinoma), and LDH correlates with tumor burden. Elevated AFP with any GCT automatically classifies it as NSGCT regardless of histology. This distinction is crucial because treatment differs: seminomas are exquisitely radiosensitive, while NSGCTs require platinum-based chemotherapy (BEP: bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin). The initial step for any solid testicular mass is radical inguinal orchiectomy — never trans-scrotal biopsy.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Pure seminoma NEVER produces AFP — any AFP elevation reclassifies the tumor as NSGCT
• C: Leydig cell tumors are sex cord-stromal tumors that may produce testosterone/estrogen but not AFP or beta-hCG
• D: Testicular lymphoma is the most common testicular malignancy in men >60, not a 25-year-old

stem: A man with testicular cancer undergoes staging CT showing retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. What is the typical lymphatic drainage pattern for testicular tumors that explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retroperitoneal para-aortic lymph nodes at the level of the renal hilum

explanation:
TESTICULAR LYMPHATIC DRAINAGE — RETROPERITONEAL NODES

Correct Answer: A — Retroperitoneal para-aortic lymph nodes at the level of the renal hilum

Testicular cancers drain to the retroperitoneal para-aortic lymph nodes because the testes develop retroperitoneally and descend during fetal life, bringing their lymphatic drainage with them via the gonadal vessels. The right testis drains primarily to the interaortocaval and pre-caval nodes, while the left testis drains to the left para-aortic and pre-aortic nodes. This is why retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) targets these specific nodal basins. Importantly, testicular tumors do NOT drain to inguinal nodes unless there has been prior scrotal surgery (orchidopexy, trans-scrotal biopsy) that altered the lymphatic pathways — this is why trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Inguinal nodes drain the scrotum, not the testis — unless prior scrotal surgery has disrupted normal lymphatics
• C: Pelvic lymph nodes receive drainage from pelvic organs (prostate, bladder), not the testes
• D: Mediastinal involvement occurs later via the thoracic duct as disease progresses, not as the primary drainage

stem: A 30-year-old man with a confirmed testicular mass is counseled about the initial surgical approach. Which procedure is standard for diagnosis and local treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Radical inguinal orchiectomy with high ligation of the spermatic cord

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trans-scrotal needle biopsy of the mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: Partial orchiectomy preserving the normal testicular tissue

explanation:
RADICAL INGUINAL ORCHIECTOMY — STANDARD OF CARE

Correct Answer: A — Radical inguinal orchiectomy with high ligation of the spermatic cord

Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the gold standard initial procedure for any solid intratesticular mass suspicious for malignancy. The inguinal approach provides early vascular control of the spermatic cord at the internal inguinal ring, preventing tumor spillage and preserving normal lymphatic drainage patterns. High ligation of the cord at the internal ring provides an adequate surgical margin and specimen for histologic staging. Serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) should be drawn pre-operatively for staging and post-operatively to confirm marker decline. Sperm banking should be offered before surgery. Staging CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis follows orchiectomy.

• B: Trans-scrotal biopsy violates the scrotal skin, risking tumor seeding to inguinal lymph nodes and altering staging/treatment
