stem: A newborn has left hydronephrosis detected on prenatal ultrasound. Postnatal ultrasound confirms SFU grade 3 hydronephrosis with preserved cortical thickness. MAG3 renal scan shows a T½ of 25 minutes (normal <20 minutes) with a differential function of 42% on the left. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
UPJ OBSTRUCTION — MOST COMMON CAUSE OF NEONATAL HYDRONEPHROSIS

Correct Answer: A — Ureteropelvic junction obstruction

UPJ obstruction is the most common cause of significant hydronephrosis in neonates, representing ~35% of prenatally detected urinary tract anomalies. The obstruction occurs at the junction of the renal pelvis and proximal ureter, most commonly from an intrinsic aperistaltic segment (smooth muscle disarrangement) or less commonly from extrinsic compression by a crossing lower pole vessel. MAG3 diuretic renography is the key functional study: a T½ (half-time of tracer washout after furosemide) >20 minutes suggests obstruction, and differential function <40% on the affected side indicates significant renal impairment. This patient with T½ 25 minutes and differential function 42% (borderline) warrants close surveillance with repeat imaging.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: PUV causes bilateral hydronephrosis with dilated/thick-walled bladder — only occurs in males and presents bilaterally
• C: VUR would show ureteral dilation on VCUG; isolated hydronephrosis without ureteral dilation suggests UPJ obstruction
• D: MCDK shows an atretic, non-functioning kidney with multiple non-communicating cysts — not hydronephrosis with preserved function

stem: A 6-month-old with left UPJ obstruction now shows differential function of 35% on the left (decreased from 42% at birth) with worsening hydronephrosis. What is the indication for surgical intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Declining differential function below 40% on serial renal scans

choices.label: B
choices.text: Any degree of hydronephrosis detected on ultrasound

choices.label: C
choices.text: T½ greater than 15 minutes on MAG3 scan

explanation:
UPJ OBSTRUCTION — SURGICAL INDICATIONS

Correct Answer: A — Declining differential function below 40% on serial renal scans

The primary indication for pyeloplasty (surgical correction of UPJ obstruction) is declining or significantly impaired renal function, defined as differential function <40% or a decrease of >5% on serial MAG3 renography. Other indications include: symptomatic obstruction (recurrent flank pain, UTIs, stones), increasing hydronephrosis with worsening drainage, and bilateral high-grade obstruction. The standard surgical repair is dismembered (Anderson-Hynes) pyeloplasty, which has a >95% success rate. Asymptomatic patients with stable differential function >40% can be managed with surveillance (serial ultrasound and renography every 3–6 months initially).

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Mild hydronephrosis alone without functional deterioration does not mandate surgery — most cases resolve spontaneously
• C: T½ >15 minutes alone is not an indication; clinical significance requires correlation with differential function and symptoms
• D: Contralateral hypertrophy is an adaptive response but not itself an indication for surgery unless the affected kidney's function is declining

stem: A child undergoes Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty for UPJ obstruction. What is the principle of this surgical technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excision of the obstructed segment and spatulated reanastomosis of the ureter to the renal pelvis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Balloon dilation of the UPJ stricture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endoscopic incision of the UPJ with laser

choices.label: D
choices.text: Placement of a permanent ureteral stent across the UPJ

explanation:
ANDERSON-HYNES DISMEMBERED PYELOPLASTY

Correct Answer: A — Excision of the obstructed segment and spatulated reanastomosis of the ureter to the renal pelvis

The Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty is the gold standard surgical repair for UPJ obstruction with >95% success rate. The technique involves: (1) complete transection of the ureter below the UPJ, (2) excision of the obstructed/aperistaltic segment, (3) reduction pyeloplasty (trimming redundant renal pelvis if dilated), (4) spatulation of the proximal ureter to create a wide anastomosis, and (5) watertight mucosa-to-mucosa reanastomosis. If a crossing vessel is identified, the ureter is transposed anterior to the vessel. The approach can be open, laparoscopic, or robotic-assisted. A temporary internal stent or external drain is typically placed to protect the anastomosis during healing.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Balloon dilation has high recurrence rates and is not standard of care for UPJ obstruction
• C: Endopyelotomy (endoscopic incision) has lower success rates (~70%) and risk of bleeding from crossing vessels
• D: Permanent stents cause encrustation, infection, and do not address the underlying obstruction

stem: A 55-year-old multiparous woman reports involuntary leakage of urine with coughing, sneezing, and lifting heavy objects. She denies urgency or frequency. Postvoid residual is 30 mL. What type of urinary incontinence is most likely?
explanation:
STRESS URINARY INCONTINENCE

Correct Answer: A — Stress urinary incontinence

Stress urinary incontinence (SUI) is the involuntary leakage of urine with activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure (coughing, sneezing, laughing, exercise, lifting). It results from urethral hypermobility (weakened pelvic floor support) or intrinsic sphincter deficiency. Risk factors include multiparity, vaginal delivery, obesity, chronic cough, and menopause (estrogen deficiency weakens urethral mucosal seal). The low PVR (30 mL) confirms adequate bladder emptying, excluding overflow incontinence. First-line treatment is pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises) for 3–6 months. Second-line options include midurethral sling procedures (TVT, TOT), which have ~80–90% success rates, or periurethral bulking agents for poor surgical candidates.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Urgency incontinence presents with sudden urge to void followed by leakage — detrusor overactivity, not related to physical activity
• C: Overflow incontinence shows high PVR (>200 mL) with continuous dribbling from bladder overdistension
• D: Functional incontinence occurs when physical or cognitive impairment prevents reaching the toilet — bladder function is normal

stem: A 70-year-old woman has urinary incontinence characterized by sudden, strong urges to urinate followed by involuntary leakage before reaching the bathroom. She also reports nocturia and frequency. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Behavioral therapy with bladder training and pelvic floor exercises

URGENCY URINARY INCONTINENCE — FIRST-LINE MANAGEMENT

Correct Answer: A — Behavioral therapy with bladder training and pelvic floor exercises

Urgency urinary incontinence (UUI/overactive bladder) is first treated with behavioral and lifestyle modifications: bladder training (timed voiding with progressive interval lengthening), pelvic floor muscle exercises, fluid management, caffeine reduction, and weight loss if applicable. These non-pharmacologic interventions are recommended first by AUA/SUFU guidelines because they are effective, have no side effects, and ~50% of patients improve significantly. Second-line therapy is antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin) or beta-3 agonists (mirabegron, vibegron). Antimuscarinics should be used cautiously in elderly patients due to cognitive side effects (especially oxybutynin). Third-line options include botulinum toxin injection into the detrusor, sacral neuromodulation, or percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation.

• B: Anticholinergics are second-line — used when behavioral therapy alone is insufficient

Correct Answer: A — The predominant/most bothersome symptom component

Treatment should target the most bothersome component first, determined by careful history and patient-reported symptom questionnaires (UDI-6, IIQ-7). If urgency predominates, behavioral therapy and anticholinergics are initiated first. If stress predominates, pelvic floor therapy followed by surgical options is prioritized. Importantly, treating one component often improves the other — for example, successful midurethral sling for stress incontinence may reduce urgency symptoms in up to 50% of MUI patients. Urodynamic testing may be considered before surgical intervention to confirm the diagnosis and guide treatment planning.

stem: A 2-year-old girl presents with her second febrile urinary tract infection. VCUG shows contrast refluxing into a dilated left ureter and renal pelvis with mild calyceal blunting (Grade III). What is the most appropriate initial management?
explanation:
VUR GRADE III — ANTIBIOTIC PROPHYLAXIS

Correct Answer: A — Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis with surveillance

Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) is graded I–V on VCUG (I: ureter only, II: ureter + non-dilated collecting system, III: mild ureteral/pelvis dilation with preserved fornices, IV: moderate dilation with blunted fornices, V: severe dilation with tortuous ureter and papillary impression loss). Low-grade VUR (I–III) has a high spontaneous resolution rate (~80% for grade I–II, ~50% for grade III) and is initially managed conservatively with continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin at 1/3 the therapeutic dose) to prevent pyelonephritis and renal scarring. Surveillance includes annual VCUG and renal ultrasound. DMSA scan assesses for renal scarring. Surgical intervention is reserved for breakthrough febrile UTIs, persistent high-grade VUR (IV–V), or renal scarring progression.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Surgery is not first-line for grade III VUR — high spontaneous resolution rate favors conservative management
• C: Endoscopic injection is a less invasive alternative to surgery but is reserved for VUR failing medical management
• D: Observation without prophylaxis may be reasonable for grade I–II in older children but not for a toddler with recurrent febrile UTIs and grade III

stem: Which embryologic mechanism explains primary vesicoureteral reflux?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abnormally short intramural ureteral tunnel through the bladder wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Posterior urethral obstruction causing bladder hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ectopic ureteral insertion into the urethra

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neurogenic bladder dysfunction from sacral agenesis

explanation:
PRIMARY VUR — EMBRYOLOGIC BASIS

Correct Answer: A — Abnormally short intramural ureteral tunnel through the bladder wall

Primary VUR results from a congenital deficiency of the intramural (submucosal) tunnel of the ureter as it passes through the bladder wall. Normally, the ureteral tunnel length-to-diameter ratio is 5:1, creating a valve mechanism where bladder filling compresses the ureteral lumen against the detrusor, preventing retrograde flow. When the tunnel is too short (lateralized ureteral orifice, often described as a "stadium" or "golf-hole" shaped orifice on cystoscopy), this flap-valve mechanism fails and urine refluxes during voiding. As the child grows, the tunnel lengthens, explaining the high spontaneous resolution rate. Primary VUR affects ~1% of children and has a 30–50% sibling recurrence rate, suggesting genetic susceptibility.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: This describes secondary VUR from bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., posterior urethral valves) — not primary VUR
• C: Ectopic ureters cause continuous urinary dribbling or obstruction — a different pathology
• D: Neurogenic dysfunction causes secondary VUR from abnormal bladder dynamics

Ref: StatPearls NBK430782 | Verified 2025

stem: A child with Grade IV VUR has breakthrough febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis and develops new renal scarring on DMSA scan. What is the most appropriate surgical intervention?
explanation:
URETERAL REIMPLANTATION FOR REFRACTORY VUR

Correct Answer: A — Ureteral reimplantation (cross-trigonal or extravesical technique)

Ureteral reimplantation is the definitive surgical treatment for VUR with a success rate >95%. The procedure creates a longer submucosal tunnel (achieving the 5:1 tunnel-to-diameter ratio) to restore the flap-valve mechanism. Two main techniques are used: (1) Cross-trigonal (Cohen) reimplant — most common intravesical approach, where the ureter is tunneled submucosally across the trigone; (2) Extravesical (Lich-Gregoir) — the ureter is embedded in a trough created in the detrusor without opening the bladder. Indications for surgery include: breakthrough febrile UTIs on prophylaxis, new/progressive renal scarring, persistent high-grade VUR (IV–V), and non-compliance with medical management. Alternative minimally invasive option: endoscopic injection of Deflux (~70–80% success rate).

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Nephrectomy removes the kidney entirely — only indicated for non-functioning, severely damaged kidneys contributing to hypertension or recurrent infections
• C: Nephrostomy is a temporizing drainage procedure for acute obstruction, not a definitive treatment for VUR
• D: Augmentation cystoplasty increases bladder capacity and is used for neurogenic bladder, not primary VUR

Ref: StatPearls NBK430782 | Verified 2025

stem: A 6-month-old girl presents with her second febrile urinary tract infection. Renal ultrasound shows mild left hydronephrosis. Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) demonstrates grade III vesicoureteral reflux on the left. What is the initial management approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis with observation for spontaneous resolution

choices.label: D
choices.text: No treatment needed — reflux always resolves spontaneously

explanation:
VESICOURETERAL REFLUX — CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT

Correct Answer: A — Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis with observation for spontaneous resolution

Primary VUR in young children has a high rate of spontaneous resolution, particularly for lower grades (I-III) and unilateral disease. The rate of resolution decreases with higher grade and bilateral involvement. For grade III VUR in a young infant, initial management is continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin) to prevent breakthrough UTIs and renal scarring while awaiting spontaneous resolution. Monitoring includes periodic renal ultrasound, VCUG or radionuclide cystogram every 12–18 months, and DMSA scan if breakthrough infections occur (to assess renal scarring). Spontaneous resolution rates: Grade I-II: ~80%; Grade III: ~50%; Grade IV-V: ~20%. Resolution occurs by maturation of the vesicoureteral junction as the intramural tunnel lengthens with bladder growth.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Surgical reimplantation is reserved for high-grade (IV-V) reflux, breakthrough infections despite prophylaxis, or progressive renal scarring
• C: Endoscopic injection (Deflux/dextranomer-hyaluronic acid) is a less-invasive alternative but typically reserved for persistent reflux or parental preference — not first-line in grade III
• D: While many cases resolve, untreated reflux with recurrent UTIs risks renal scarring and reflux nephropathy — prophylaxis is warranted

Ref: StatPearls NBK441481 | Verified 2025

stem: On VCUG, reflux is graded based on the degree of ureteral and collecting system dilation. Which finding corresponds to Grade V (most severe) vesicoureteral reflux?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reflux into the ureter only without dilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reflux with gross ureteral dilation, tortuous ureter, and loss of papillary impressions in the calyces

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reflux into the renal pelvis without calyceal dilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reflux into a mildly dilated ureter with preserved calyceal fornices

explanation:
VUR GRADING — INTERNATIONAL REFLUX STUDY CLASSIFICATION

Correct Answer: B — Reflux with gross ureteral dilation, tortuous ureter, and loss of papillary impressions

The International Reflux Study Committee grading system (I-V) on VCUG: Grade I — reflux into non-dilated ureter only (no renal pelvis filling); Grade II — reflux into renal pelvis and calyces without dilation, preserved calyceal fornices; Grade III — mild to moderate ureteral dilation, mild calyceal blunting; Grade IV — moderate ureteral dilation with tortuosity, moderate calyceal blunting but papillary impressions still visible; Grade V — gross ureteral dilation and tortuosity, severe calyceal dilation with LOSS of papillary impressions (clubbed calyces), and possible intrarenal reflux. Grading guides management: Grades I-III → conservative; Grades IV-V → more likely to require surgical intervention due to low spontaneous resolution rates and higher risk of renal damage.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• A: Reflux into ureter only without dilation describes Grade I
• C: Reflux into renal pelvis without dilation describes Grade II
• D: Mild ureteral dilation with preserved fornices describes Grade III

Ref: StatPearls NBK441481 | Verified 2025

stem: A child with recurrent febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis has Grade IV bilateral VUR and new renal scarring on DMSA scan. The parents ask about surgical options. Which surgical approach has the highest success rate?
explanation:
SURGICAL CORRECTION OF VUR — URETERAL REIMPLANTATION

Correct Answer: A — Open ureteral reimplantation (Cohen cross-trigonal technique)

Open ureteral reimplantation remains the gold standard for surgical correction of VUR, with success rates of 95–98% across all grades. The Cohen cross-trigonal technique creates a longer submucosal tunnel for the ureter across the trigone (tunnel length-to-diameter ratio of 5:1 creates a flap-valve mechanism preventing reflux). Indications for surgery include: breakthrough UTIs despite prophylaxis, progressive renal scarring, high-grade (IV-V) bilateral reflux, non-compliance with prophylaxis, or family/patient preference. Endoscopic injection (Deflux) is a minimally invasive alternative with ~70–80% success per injection for grades II-IV but lower for grade V. The procedure can be repeated but cumulative success still falls below open reimplantation. Robotic-assisted reimplantation is an emerging alternative with similar success rates.

Why other answers are incorrect:
• B: Deflux has lower success rates (~70–80% vs 95–98%) especially for Grade IV — and this patient has failed conservative management
• C: Nephrectomy is inappropriate — the kidneys are functional with scarring but not end-stage
• D: Ureteral stents do not correct the underlying anatomic defect causing reflux

Ref: StatPearls NBK441481 | Verified 2025

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents at 5 AM with sudden-onset severe left testicular pain that woke him from sleep 3 hours ago. He has nausea and one episode of vomiting. No dysuria, no fever, no urethral discharge. He reports a similar brief episode 2 weeks ago that resolved spontaneously.Vital SignsTemp 37.0°CHR 108ExamLeft testis High-riding, horizontally oriented (bell-clapper deformity), extremely tenderCremasteric reflex ABSENT on leftPrehn sign Negative (elevation does NOT relieve pain)Right testis Normal, cremasteric reflex present What is the diagnosis, and what is the critical time window?

choices.text: Epididymitis — treat with antibiotics and scrotal elevation
Testicular torsion — the spermatic cord twists, cutting off blood supply;
Order Doppler ultrasound before any surgical consultation
Torsion of the testicular appendage — conservative management

explanation:
Correct Answer: Testicular torsion = urologic EMERGENCY. The spermatic cord twists, occluding the testicular artery → ischemia. TIME IS TESTIS: salvage rates: 12-24 hours = 10-20%; >24 hours = near 0%. Clinical findings: sudden-onset severe testicular pain, high-riding testis, horizontal lie (bell-clapper deformity), ABSENT cremasteric reflex (most sensitive physical finding, ~99% sensitivity), negative Prehn sign (elevation doesn't relieve pain). Do NOT delay for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high — go directly to OR. Prior intermittent episode (2 weeks ago) = intermittent torsion-detorsion (increases urgency for surgical fixation). Treatment: emergent surgical detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy (fix BOTH testes — bell-clapper deformity is typically bilateral).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Adolescent male + sudden onset testicular pain from sleep = peak torsion scenario
- High-riding, horizontally oriented testis = bell-clapper deformity (tunica vaginalis attachment allows free rotation)
- Absent cremasteric reflex = most sensitive finding for torsion (~99%)
- No fever, no dysuria = NOT infectious (epididymitis would have fever + pyuria)
- Prior similar episode = intermittent torsion-detorsion (bell-clapper deformity confirmed)
- 3 hours from onset = within the 6-hour golden window → go to OR immediately

Evidence: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - Sharp VJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK448199. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Epididymitis: Epididymitis causes gradual-onset scrotal pain (days, not sudden), fever, dysuria, and pyuria. Cremasteric reflex is PRESENT and Prehn sign is POSITIVE (elevation relieves pain) in epididymitis. This patient has sudden onset, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn sign, no urinary symptoms, and no fever = torsion, not epididymitis.
- Doppler ultrasound first: While Doppler US is highly sensitive (82-100%), it should NOT delay surgery when clinical suspicion is high. This patient has ALL classic torsion findings (sudden onset, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis, horizontal lie). US may produce false negatives (incomplete torsion) and wastes precious time within the 6-hour window. Sensitivity of clinical diagnosis by experienced clinician approaches US.
- Torsion of testicular appendage (appendix testis): Appendix testis torsion (torsion of vestigial Mullerian duct remnant) causes scrotal pain but is LESS severe, has a characteristic "blue dot sign" (visible through scrotal skin at upper pole of testis), PRESERVED cremasteric reflex, and is managed conservatively (self-limited). This patient's severity, absent cremasteric reflex, and high-riding testis indicate spermatic cord torsion.
- Incarcerated inguinal hernia: Incarcerated hernia causes inguinoscrotal swelling with bowel contents palpable in the inguinal canal, often with signs of bowel obstruction (abdominal distension, vomiting). It does NOT typically cause an isolated high-riding testis with absent cremasteric reflex.

Reference: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448199/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 38-year-old man presents with sudden-onset severe left flank pain radiating to the left groin. The pain is colicky (waxing and waning), and he is writhing and unable to find a comfortable position. He has nausea and one episode of vomiting. No fever.Vital SignsTemp 37.1°CHR 104BP 148/92LabsUA 20-50 RBC/hpf (microscopic hematuria), pH 5.5, no WBC, no bacteriaBMP Cr 1.0, Ca 10.2CT abdomen 5mm stone at left ureterovesical junction (UVJ) with proximal hydroureteronephrosis What is the management based on stone size and location?

choices.text: Emergency surgery for all kidney stones
5mm stone at UVJ — high likelihood of spontaneous passage (~50-70%);
All stones pass spontaneously regardless of size
CT scan is unnecessary — ultrasound is adequate for all stone evaluations
Start antibiotics empirically for possible urinary tract infection

explanation:
Correct Answer: Nephrolithiasis management by stone size: : ~80% pass spontaneously; observe + pain control. 4-6mm: ~50% pass; medical expulsive therapy (tamsulosin) + observe 4-6 weeks. 6-10mm: ~10-20% pass; consider intervention. >10mm: unlikely to pass; requires intervention. Pain management: IV ketorolac (NSAID) is FIRST-LINE for renal colic (superior to opioids for colicky pain; also reduces ureteral spasm). Tamsulosin (alpha-1 blocker): relaxes ureteral smooth muscle (especially distal ureter), increases passage rate by 30%. CT non-contrast is gold standard for stone detection (sensitivity >95%). Emergent intervention needed if: infected/obstructed kidney (pyonephrosis = EMERGENCY), solitary kidney with obstruction, bilateral obstruction, intractable pain/vomiting.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden colicky flank-to-groin pain + hematuria = renal colic (classic)
- 5mm stone at UVJ = distal location (favorable for passage) but borderline size
- No fever + no WBC in urine = no infection (critical distinction)
- UVJ: narrowest point of ureter (along with UPJ and iliac vessel crossing)
- Tamsulosin: medical expulsive therapy (relaxes distal ureteral smooth muscle)
- Follow-up: if not passed in 4-6 weeks, proceed to ureteroscopy or lithotripsy

Evidence: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - Thakore P, et al. StatPearls. NBK455921. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Emergency surgery for all kidney stones: Most kidney stones pass spontaneously (especially 10mm (unlikely to pass), stones that don't pass within 4-6 weeks, infected obstructed kidneys, intractable pain/vomiting, bilateral obstruction, or solitary kidney obstruction.
- All stones pass spontaneously: Stone passage depends on SIZE: 10mm (<5%). This 5mm stone has a reasonable but not guaranteed chance of passage. Medical expulsive therapy (tamsulosin) improves the odds.
- CT scan unnecessary: Ultrasound can detect hydronephrosis and some stones, but CT non-contrast is the gold standard with >95% sensitivity and >98% specificity. CT provides critical information: stone size, location, number, and alternative diagnoses. US misses ureteral stones and small stones. US is first-line in pregnancy (avoid radiation).
- Start empiric antibiotics: There is NO evidence of infection: no fever (37.1°C), no WBC in urine, no bacteria. Antibiotics are unnecessary and inappropriate without infection. However, if fever + obstructing stone were present, this would be an infected obstructed kidney requiring EMERGENT decompression + IV antibiotics.

Reference: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK455921/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 27-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of a painless, firm mass in his right testicle that he noticed while showering. He also reports a dull ache in the right hemiscrotum and notes that the testicle feels heavier than usual. He has a history of right cryptorchidism (undescended testis) corrected with orchiopexy at age 2.ExamRight testis Firm, non-tender, 3 cm solid mass; does NOT transilluminateLeft testis NormalInguinal LN None palpableLabsβ-hCG Mildly elevatedAFP Markedly elevatedLDH 280 U/L (mildly elevated)Scrotal US 3.2 cm solid intratesticular mass, increased vascularity What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the initial surgical approach?

choices.text: Testicular germ cell tumor (likely mixed GCT or yolk sac component given elevated AFP) — initial
Varicocele — dilated pampiniform plexus (“bag of worms”)
Trans-scrotal biopsy to determine histology before treatment planning

explanation:
Correct Answer: Testicular cancer: most common solid malignancy in men 15-35. A painless, firm testicular mass that does NOT transilluminate = cancer until proven otherwise. Tumor markers: AFP (yolk sac tumor, mixed GCT — NEVER elevated in pure seminoma), β-hCG (choriocarcinoma, can be mildly elevated in seminoma), LDH (bulk of disease). CRITICAL: the initial surgical approach is RADICAL INGUINAL ORCHIECTOMY (through an inguinal incision, clamping the spermatic cord at the internal ring BEFORE manipulating the testis). NEVER perform trans-scrotal biopsy (disrupts scrotal lymphatic drainage, which is different from testicular → risks scrotal skin/inguinal node metastasis). Risk factors: cryptorchidism (#1, 4-8x risk even after orchiopexy), prior testicular cancer, family history, Klinefelter syndrome.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 27-year-old male + painless testicular mass = testicular cancer until proven otherwise
- Firm, solid, non-transilluminating = solid mass (not hydrocele which transilluminates)
- AFP markedly elevated = non-seminomatous component (yolk sac tumor or mixed GCT)
- History of cryptorchidism = #1 risk factor for testicular cancer (4-8x increased risk)
- Scrotal US: solid intratesticular mass with increased vascularity = highly suspicious for malignancy
- RADICAL INGUINAL orchiectomy (NOT trans-scrotal) = standard initial approach

Evidence: Testicular Cancer - StatPearls - Baird DC, et al. StatPearls. NBK563159. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Epididymal cyst: Epididymal cysts are located in the epididymis (SEPARATE from the testis), are typically soft and cystic, and transilluminate. This mass is within the testicle (intratesticular), firm, and does NOT transilluminate = solid mass, not a cyst.
- Hydrocele: Hydroceles are fluid collections around the testis that TRANSILLUMINATE (light passes through the fluid). This mass does NOT transilluminate and is solid on ultrasound. Hydroceles are non-tender and diffusely surround the testis; this is a focal firm mass.
- Varicocele: Varicoceles are dilated veins of the pampiniform plexus, classically described as a "bag of worms" that increases with Valsalva and decompresses when supine. They are typically LEFT-sided (due to left gonadal vein drainage into left renal vein). This is a firm solid mass in the RIGHT testis with elevated tumor markers.
- Trans-scrotal biopsy: Trans-scrotal biopsy or orchiectomy is CONTRAINDICATED because it violates the scrotal skin and disrupts lymphatic drainage patterns. Testicular lymphatics drain to retroperitoneal nodes (para-aortic), while scrotal skin drains to INGUINAL nodes. Trans-scrotal approach risks seeding inguinal nodes and scrotal skin, changing staging and requiring additional treatment.

Reference: Testicular Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563159/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with inability to urinate for 12 hours despite feeling an intense urge. He has a history of progressive urinary symptoms over 2 years: weak stream, hesitancy, nocturia (4x/night), incomplete emptying, and terminal dribbling. He takes no medications. Exam shows a distended, palpable bladder. Digital rectal exam reveals a smooth, symmetrically enlarged, firm, non-tender prostate.Vital SignsBP 158/94HR 92LabsBladder scan Post-void residual: 850 mLBMP Cr 1.8 (baseline 1.0)PSA 4.2 ng/mL (mildly elevated, proportional to gland size)UA Negative What is the immediate intervention, and what are the two medication classes for ongoing management?

choices.text: Acute urinary retention from BPH — immediate FOLEY CATHETER decompression (relieve obstruction);
Intermittent catheterization only, no medications needed
Immediate surgical intervention (TURP) without medical management trial
Start antibiotics for presumed UTI causing retention

explanation:
Correct Answer: BPH causing acute urinary retention: immediate Foley catheter decompression is REQUIRED. Post-obstructive diuresis: after relieving long-standing obstruction, massive urine output (>200 mL/h) can occur due to osmotic diuresis from accumulated urea + impaired tubular concentrating ability. Can cause severe dehydration, hypokalemia, hyponatremia — replace 50-75% of output with IV NS and monitor electrolytes q6h. Elevated creatinine (1.8 from 1.0) = obstructive nephropathy (hydronephrosis from chronic retention). BPH medications: (1) Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin): relax smooth muscle in prostate and bladder neck → immediate symptomatic improvement (days). (2) 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride): block conversion of testosterone to DHT → shrink prostate 20-30% over 6-12 months. Combination therapy superior for large prostates (>30g).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 12 hours unable to void + palpable distended bladder = acute urinary retention
- Smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate on DRE = BPH (not cancer, which is hard/nodular)
- 2-year progressive LUTS (weak stream, hesitancy, nocturia, dribbling) = BPH symptoms
- Cr elevated from 1.0 to 1.8 = obstructive nephropathy from chronic retention
- Post-void residual 850 mL = significant retention (normal <100 mL)
- Foley immediately, then dual medical therapy for BPH

Evidence: BPH - StatPearls - McVary KT, et al. StatPearls. NBK558920. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Prostate biopsy first: Prostate biopsy is indicated for suspected prostate CANCER (hard/nodular prostate, elevated PSA disproportionate to gland size, abnormal MRI). This patient has a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate consistent with BPH, and his PSA (4.2) is proportional to prostate size. The immediate priority is relieving his acute retention with a catheter.
- Intermittent catheterization only: While intermittent catheterization can be considered for long-term management, it does NOT address the underlying BPH causing progressive obstruction. This patient needs BOTH catheter decompression (immediate) AND pharmacologic management (alpha-blocker + 5-ARI) to prevent recurrence. Without medication, retention will recur.
- Immediate TURP without medical trial: AUA/CUA guidelines recommend a trial of medical management before surgical intervention for BPH, except in specific indications for surgery: refractory retention despite catheter trials, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, renal insufficiency not improving with catheterization, or failed medical therapy. This patient should have a catheter trial + medications first.
- Start antibiotics for UTI: The UA is NEGATIVE (no WBC, no bacteria, no nitrites). There is no evidence of UTI. The retention is from mechanical obstruction (BPH), not infection. Starting antibiotics without evidence of infection contributes to antibiotic resistance.

Reference: BPH - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK558920/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents after noticing painless gross hematuria (blood in his urine without any pain). He has no dysuria, no flank pain, no fever. The hematuria has occurred intermittently over 3 weeks. UA shows >50 RBC/hpf with no WBC or bacteria.Vital SignsBP 136/82HR 76Temp 37.0°CLabsUA >50 RBC/hpf, no WBC, no bacteria, no castsCr 1.1CBC Normal What is the most concerning diagnosis, and what is the complete workup?

choices.text: UTI — start antibiotics based on hematuria alone
Painless gross hematuria in a 62-year-old smoker = BLADDER CANCER until proven otherwise
Kidney stone is the most likely cause — CT non-contrast only
BPH with hematuria — start alpha-blocker and observe
Repeat UA in 3 months — transient hematuria is usually benign

explanation:
Correct Answer: Painless gross hematuria in adults >40 = malignancy until proven otherwise. Bladder cancer is the #1 cause of painless gross hematuria in adults (especially males, smokers, >50 years). The complete hematuria workup includes: (1) Cystoscopy: direct visualization of the bladder mucosa (gold standard for bladder tumors). (2) CT urogram (CT with IV contrast, delayed images): evaluates upper tracts (renal cell carcinoma, ureteral tumors, stones). (3) Urine cytology: high specificity (~95%) for high-grade urothelial carcinoma but low sensitivity for low-grade tumors. Key principle: painless hematuria = worry about cancer; PAINFUL hematuria = think about stones, infection. Risk factors for bladder cancer: smoking (#1, 4x risk), occupational chemical exposure (arylamines: rubber, dye, textile workers), chronic cystitis, cyclophosphamide, pelvic radiation.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 62-year-old male + 40-pack-year smoker = very high-risk for bladder cancer
- Painless gross hematuria = #1 presenting symptom of bladder cancer (80-90%)
- No dysuria, no flank pain, no fever = NOT infection or stone (which cause pain)
- No WBC or bacteria in UA = no infection
- Intermittent over 3 weeks = concerning for malignancy (not a one-time event)
- Complete workup needed: cystoscopy + CT urogram + cytology

Evidence: Bladder Cancer/Hematuria - StatPearls - Lenis AT, et al. StatPearls. NBK470189. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- UTI — antibiotics: There are NO WBC or bacteria in the UA, definitively ruling out UTI. Hematuria alone without pyuria or bacteriuria does NOT indicate UTI. Starting antibiotics without infection evidence is inappropriate and delays the cancer workup.
- Kidney stone — CT non-contrast: Kidney stones cause PAINFUL hematuria (renal colic: severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin). This patient has PAINLESS hematuria. While CT should evaluate the upper tracts, a CT UROGRAM (with contrast) is needed for the complete hematuria workup, not just a non-contrast stone protocol. Additionally, cystoscopy is essential to evaluate the bladder.
- BPH — alpha-blocker and observe: While BPH can cause hematuria, EVERY episode of gross hematuria in adults >40 requires a complete workup to rule out malignancy, even if BPH is present. Attributing hematuria to BPH without cystoscopy and imaging is a dangerous assumption that can miss bladder cancer.
- Repeat UA in 3 months: Delaying workup for 3 months in a high-risk patient (smoker, >60, gross hematuria) risks delayed cancer diagnosis. The AUA recommends IMMEDIATE complete workup for gross hematuria. Even MICROSCOPIC hematuria in high-risk patients warrants prompt evaluation.

Reference: Hematuria Workup - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470189/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 56-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes (A1c 11.2%) and obesity presents with 3 days of progressive perineal pain, swelling, and erythema that has spread rapidly overnight. He now has fever, tachycardia, and the scrotal/perineal skin is dusky with crepitus on palpation. A foul-smelling discharge is present.Vital SignsTemp 39.8°CHR 128BP 82/54LabsWBC 24,500 with 18% bandsLactate 5.2 mmol/LCr 2.4Glucose 428 mg/dLCT pelvis Soft tissue gas tracking along perineal fascial planes What is the diagnosis, and what is the life-saving intervention?

choices.text: Scrotal cellulitis — IV antibiotics alone are sufficient
Fournier gangrene — necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum/genitalia;
Perineal abscess — incision and drainage of a single abscess cavity
Stabilize with IV fluids and schedule surgery for tomorrow morning

explanation:
Correct Answer: Fournier gangrene: necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum, scrotum, and/or penis. SURGICAL EMERGENCY: emergent radical debridement is the ONLY life-saving intervention. Antibiotics are necessary but ADJUNCTIVE — they cannot penetrate necrotic, avascular tissue. Mortality: 20-40% overall; approaches 100% without surgery; every hour of surgical delay increases mortality by 7.5%. Key findings: pain out of proportion to exam (early), crepitus (subcutaneous gas from anaerobic bacteria), dusky/necrotic skin, rapid spread beyond visible skin changes (fascial necrosis extends far beyond skin findings), septic shock. Risk factors: diabetes (60-70% of cases), obesity, immunosuppression, alcoholism, perineal trauma/surgery. Polymicrobial: aerobic (E. coli, GAS, S. aureus) + anaerobic (Bacteroides, Clostridium). Debridement: all necrotic tissue must be excised (often requires multiple return trips to OR for re-debridement).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Perineal pain + swelling spreading rapidly + crepitus = necrotizing fasciitis
- CT showing soft tissue gas in fascial planes = gas gangrene (anaerobic infection)
- Septic shock (BP 82/54, HR 128, lactate 5.2) = systemic toxicity
- Diabetes (A1c 11.2%) = #1 risk factor for Fournier gangrene
- Dusky skin + foul-smelling discharge = tissue necrosis (beyond rescue with antibiotics alone)
- Every hour of delay = 7.5% increased mortality → go to OR NOW

Evidence: Fournier Gangrene - StatPearls - Chennamsetty A, et al. StatPearls. NBK549821. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Scrotal cellulitis — IV antibiotics alone: Cellulitis causes erythema and warmth but does NOT cause crepitus, soft tissue gas, dusky/necrotic skin, or rapid fascial spread. The presence of crepitus and CT showing gas in fascial planes indicates necrotizing fasciitis, which CANNOT be treated with antibiotics alone. Necrotic tissue is avascular — antibiotics cannot reach the infection.
- Perineal abscess — simple I&D: A simple perineal abscess is a localized collection that can be drained with a limited I&D. Fournier gangrene involves DIFFUSE fascial necrosis spreading along tissue planes — it is not a single collection. It requires RADICAL debridement of all necrotic tissue, often involving wide excision of scrotal skin, subcutaneous tissue, and fascia.
- Epididymo-orchitis: Epididymo-orchitis causes scrotal pain with a tender, swollen epididymis, fever, and dysuria. It does NOT cause crepitus, soft tissue gas, necrotic skin, or septic shock. The findings here (crepitus, gas on CT, rapid spread, shock) are pathognomonic for necrotizing fasciitis, not orchitis.
- Stabilize and schedule surgery tomorrow: DELAY IS DEATH. Every hour of surgical delay increases mortality by 7.5% in Fournier gangrene. This patient is in septic shock with clear necrotizing infection. Surgery must occur EMERGENTLY (within hours of diagnosis), not the next morning. Stabilization (fluids, antibiotics, vasopressors) should occur simultaneously with OR preparation, not instead of surgery.

Reference: Fournier Gangrene - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549821/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 55-year-old African American man asks about prostate cancer screening at his annual physical. He has no urinary symptoms. His father was diagnosed with prostate cancer at age 60. DRE reveals a firm, asymmetric, irregular nodule in the right posterior lobe of the prostate.LabsPSA 8.4 ng/mL (normal Free PSA 8% (low free-to-total ratio) What do the DRE and PSA findings indicate, and what is the next step?

choices.text: PSA 8.4 is due to BPH — start alpha-blocker
Hard, irregular, asymmetric nodule on DRE + elevated PSA with low free PSA ratio (≤10%
Repeat PSA in 6 months before any further workup
CT abdomen/pelvis to stage before biopsy
PSA screening is never recommended for any patient

explanation:
Correct Answer: Prostate cancer screening and workup: DRE finding of a hard, irregular, asymmetric nodule is suspicious for cancer (vs BPH: smooth, symmetric, rubbery). PSA: >4.0 ng/mL warrants further evaluation; >10 = high suspicion. Free PSA ratio: cancer cells produce more bound PSA; free-to-total  for cancer; >25% = more likely BPH. Next step: tissue diagnosis via biopsy (TRUS-guided systematic biopsy or MRI-TRUS fusion targeted biopsy). Prostate cancer zones: cancer arises in the peripheral zone (70%, palpable on DRE); BPH arises in the transition zone (periurethral). High-risk populations: African American men (2x incidence, 2x mortality), family history (first-degree relative = 2-3x risk). Screening guidelines: shared decision-making starting at 55 (general) or 40-45 (African American or family history).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Hard, irregular, asymmetric nodule = concerning for cancer (peripheral zone)
- PSA 8.4 = elevated above 4.0 threshold
- Free PSA 8% = low ratio, highly suspicious for cancer (cancer produces more bound PSA)
- African American + father with prostate cancer = two strong risk factors
- DRE + PSA + risk factors = tissue diagnosis required
- TRUS-guided biopsy or MRI-fusion biopsy is the next step

Evidence: Prostate Cancer Screening - StatPearls - Leslie SW, et al. StatPearls. NBK470362. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- PSA 8.4 due to BPH: While BPH elevates PSA, the DRE finding of a hard, IRREGULAR, ASYMMETRIC nodule is NOT consistent with BPH (which is smooth, symmetric, rubbery). The low free PSA ratio (8%) further supports cancer over BPH. BPH causes PSA elevation proportional to gland size, but free PSA is typically >25% in BPH.
- Repeat PSA in 6 months: Delaying workup with an abnormal DRE + elevated PSA + low free PSA + high-risk demographics (African American, family history) is inappropriate. An abnormal DRE ALONE is an indication for biopsy regardless of PSA level. Waiting 6 months risks missing aggressive cancer.
- CT before biopsy: CT does NOT diagnose prostate cancer — tissue biopsy is required. CT is used for STAGING after diagnosis (looking for lymph node and distant metastases) in high-risk cases. The diagnostic pathway is: suspicious DRE/PSA → mpMRI (optional, for biopsy targeting) → prostate biopsy.
- PSA screening never recommended: While PSA screening is controversial, major guidelines (AUA, USPSTF) recommend shared decision-making for men 55-69 (and earlier for high-risk groups: African American, family history). The USPSTF changed from a D recommendation (against) to a C recommendation (shared decision-making) in 2018. For this high-risk patient, screening is appropriate and has detected a suspicious finding.

Reference: Prostate Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470362/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 22-year-old sexually active man presents with 3 days of gradually worsening left scrotal pain and swelling. He reports dysuria and urethral discharge that started before the scrotal symptoms. Temperature is 38.4°C. Exam shows a swollen, tender LEFT epididymis with overlying scrotal erythema.Vital SignsTemp 38.4°CExamLeft epididymis Swollen, tender, induratedCremasteric reflex PRESENTPrehn sign POSITIVE (elevation relieves pain)Urinalysis Positive for leukocyte esterase, 20 WBC/hpfNAAT Pending (chlamydia/gonorrhea) How does this differ from testicular torsion?

choices.text: Testicular torsion — absent cremasteric reflex requires emergent surgery
Acute epididymitis — differs from torsion in EVERY key clinical feature:

explanation:
Correct Answer: Epididymitis vs testicular torsion — the most critical scrotal pain differential:

| Feature | Epididymitis | Torsion |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| Onset | Gradual (days) | Sudden (minutes) |
| Fever | Present | Absent |
| Urinary Sx | Dysuria, discharge | None |
| Cremasteric | PRESENT | ABSENT |
| Prehn sign | POSITIVE (relief) | NEGATIVE |
| UA | Pyuria | Normal |
| Age peak | 18-35 (STI), >35 (enteric) | 12-18 |

Treatment by age: : ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 + doxycycline 100mg BID x 10 days (covers N. gonorrhoeae + C. trachomatis). >35 or MSM with insertive anal sex: levofloxacin 500mg daily x 10 days (covers enteric gram-negatives). If torsion cannot be excluded clinically: get Doppler ultrasound immediately (do NOT delay for UA results).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Gradual onset over 3 DAYS (not sudden) = epididymitis pattern
- Dysuria + urethral discharge PRECEDING scrotal pain = ascending infection
- Fever 38.4°C = infection (torsion is typically afebrile)
- Cremasteric reflex PRESENT = most sensitive distinction from torsion
- Prehn sign POSITIVE = elevation relieves pain (inflammation, not ischemia)
- Pyuria (20 WBC) = urinary tract infection / STI-associated
- 22-year-old sexually active = Chlamydia/GC most likely organisms

Evidence: Epididymitis - StatPearls - Taylor SN, et al. StatPearls. NBK430814. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Testicular torsion: Every feature in this case points AWAY from torsion: gradual onset (days, not sudden), fever (infection, not ischemia), dysuria/discharge (ascending STI), cremasteric reflex present (absent in torsion), Prehn sign positive (negative in torsion), and pyuria (normal UA in torsion). However, if ANY doubt exists, Doppler US should be obtained immediately.
- Testicular cancer: Testicular cancer presents as a PAINLESS, firm testicular mass. It does not cause fever, dysuria, urethral discharge, or epididymal tenderness. The tender swollen epididymis with infectious symptoms is diagnostic of epididymitis.
- Hydrocele: Hydrocele is a painless fluid collection around the testis that TRANSILLUMINATES. It does not cause fever, dysuria, or tenderness. This patient has a tender, indurated epididymis with systemic signs of infection.
- Orchitis alone: Isolated orchitis (without epididymitis) is uncommon in adults. Mumps orchitis is the classic exception (post-pubertal males with mumps). This patient's tender epididymis, dysuria, and discharge indicate epididymitis (which may extend to the testis as epididymo-orchitis).

Reference: Epididymitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430814/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 71-year-old man with a history of BPH (on tamsulosin) presents to the ED with sudden inability to urinate for the past 10 hours. He reports suprapubic pressure and discomfort. He took an over-the-counter cold medication containing pseudoephedrine and diphenhydramine yesterday. His prostate has been gradually enlarging per his urologist.Vital SignsBP 158/92HR 88ExamSuprapubic Firm, distended, tender bladder palpable to umbilicusDRE Smoothly enlarged prostate, no nodulesBladder scan 850 mL post-void residual (unable to void) What is the immediate management and what precipitated this episode?

choices.text: Schedule a urology appointment for next week
Acute urinary retention — IMMEDIATE bladder decompression via urethral catheterization (or
Fluid restriction will allow the bladder to decompress naturally
Start antibiotics for urinary tract infection

explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute urinary retention (AUR): inability to void with a distended, palpable bladder. IMMEDIATE catheterization is required (urethral Foley preferred; suprapubic if urethral not possible). Medication-precipitated AUR in BPH: multiple drug classes impair voiding: (1) α-agonists (pseudoephedrine, phenylephrine): contract bladder neck/prostatic smooth muscle → increased outlet resistance. (2) Anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, first-gen antihistamines, tricyclics): decrease detrusor muscle contractility. (3) Opioids: decrease detrusor contractility + increase sphincter tone. In BPH patients, these medications convert compensated obstruction into acute retention. Post-decompression: monitor for post-obstructive diuresis (>200 mL/h for 2+ hours after catheterization → IV fluid replacement to prevent dehydration). Trial without catheter (TWOC) in 1-3 days after starting/optimizing alpha-blocker.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 71-year-old male with BPH + unable to void for 10 hours = acute urinary retention
- Bladder palpable to umbilicus with 850 mL = significantly distended
- Pseudoephedrine (α-agonist) = contracts bladder neck, increases outlet resistance
- Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) = reduces detrusor contractility
- Double hit: increased resistance + decreased contractile force = retention
- Immediate catheterization to decompress; do NOT delay

Evidence: Urinary Retention - StatPearls - Selius BA, et al. StatPearls. NBK538544. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Urology appointment next week: Acute urinary retention with 850 mL in the bladder is an EMERGENCY requiring immediate decompression. Delayed treatment risks bladder damage (overdistension injury to detrusor muscle), renal impairment (bilateral hydronephrosis from backpressure), and UTI/urosepsis from stagnant urine.
- Fluid restriction: Fluid restriction will NOT resolve mechanical obstruction. The bladder cannot empty because the outlet is obstructed (BPH + medication effects), not because of excess fluid input. The 850 mL already in the bladder must be drained mechanically via catheterization.
- Start antibiotics: There is no evidence of UTI in this presentation (no fever, no dysuria before retention, no mention of pyuria). The precipitant was medication (pseudoephedrine + diphenhydramine). Antibiotics may be indicated if catheterization reveals infected urine, but the immediate intervention is bladder decompression.
- Emergent prostatectomy: Surgery (TURP) is NOT emergent in this situation. The immediate treatment is catheterization + medication optimization (alpha-blocker + discontinue offending drugs) + trial without catheter. Surgery is considered for patients who fail medical management or have recurrent retention. TURP is elective/semi-urgent, not emergent.

Reference: Urinary Retention - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538544/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old male smoker presents with painless gross hematuria. He also reports a dull left flank ache and unintentional 15-lb weight loss over 3 months. He noticed a new left-sided scrotal swelling that does not reduce when lying down. Exam confirms a left varicocele that fails to decompress in the supine position.ExamLeft flank Palpable massLeft scrotum Varicocele that does NOT decompress supineLabs Hgb 18.2 g/dL (erythrocytosis), Ca 11.8 mg/dL (hypercalcemia)CT abdomen0 11 02 .3 54  5 c6 m7  8 e9 n10 h11 a12 n13 c14 i15 n16 g17  18 l19 e20 f21 t22  23 r24 e25 n26 a27 l28  29 m30 a31 s32 s33  34 w35 i36 t37 h38  39 e40 x41 t42 e43 n44 s45 i46 o47 n48  49 i50 n51 t52 o53  54 l55 e56 f57 t58  59 r60 e61 n62 a63 l64  65 v66 e67 i68 n69 ;70  71 l72 e73 f74 t75 -76 s77 i78 d79 e80 d81  82 v83 a84 r85 i86 c87 o88 c89 e90 l91 e92  93 s94 e95 c96 o97 n98 d99 a100 r101 y102  103 t104 o105  106 r107 e108 n109 a110 l111  112 v113 e114 i115 n116  117 c118 o119 m120 p121 r122 e123 s124 s125 i126 o127 n What is the most likely diagnosis, and why is the non-reducing varicocele significant?

choices.text: Renal cyst — simple cyst requiring no treatment
Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) — the classic triad (hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass) is present in only
Transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis — urothelial cancer
Polycystic kidney disease — bilateral enlarged kidneys

explanation:
Correct Answer: Renal cell carcinoma: the “internist’s tumor” because of diverse paraneoplastic syndromes. Classic triad (hematuria + flank pain + palpable mass) is present in only 10-15% but indicates advanced disease. Non-reducing LEFT varicocele: the left gonadal vein drains into the LEFT RENAL VEIN (not directly into IVC like the right). Tumor invading the left renal vein compresses the gonadal vein outflow → varicocele that does NOT decompress supine (a normal varicocele decompresses supine because gravity relieves venous congestion). This is a RED FLAG for left renal malignancy. Paraneoplastic syndromes: erythrocytosis (ectopic EPO, 3-10%), hypercalcemia (PTHrP, 13-20%), Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without liver metastasis, 10-15%), hypertension (ectopic renin). Treatment: partial or radical nephrectomy; RCC is notoriously resistant to chemotherapy and radiation; immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors: nivolumab, pembrolizumab) + TKI (sunitinib, cabozantinib) for metastatic disease.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Painless gross hematuria in 58-year-old = malignancy until proven otherwise
- Classic triad: hematuria + flank pain + palpable mass (present = advanced disease)
- Left varicocele that does NOT decompress supine = renal vein invasion (pathognomonic)
- Hgb 18.2 = erythrocytosis (paraneoplastic EPO production)
- Ca 11.8 = hypercalcemia (paraneoplastic PTHrP)
- 15-lb weight loss = constitutional symptoms of malignancy
- CT: 10.5 cm enhancing mass with renal vein extension confirms RCC

Evidence: RCC - StatPearls - Gray RE, et al. StatPearls. NBK470336. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Renal cyst: Simple renal cysts are anechoic (non-enhancing) on imaging with thin smooth walls. This mass is 10.5 cm, ENHANCING (solid tissue with blood supply), and invading the renal vein. Enhancing renal masses are malignant until proven otherwise. Bosniak classification: Bosniak I-II = benign cysts; Bosniak III-IV = likely malignant, require surgery.
- Transitional cell carcinoma (TCC): TCC of the renal pelvis can cause hematuria but typically appears as a filling defect in the renal pelvis on imaging (not a large parenchymal mass). TCC does not typically cause erythrocytosis or hypercalcemia (these are RCC paraneoplastic syndromes). The enhancing renal parenchymal mass with renal vein invasion is characteristic of RCC.
- BPH: BPH can cause hematuria, but it does NOT cause a palpable flank mass, renal vein invasion, varicocele, erythrocytosis, or hypercalcemia. BPH causes lower urinary tract symptoms (frequency, hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia). The CT findings of a renal mass are not related to BPH.
- Polycystic kidney disease: PKD causes BILATERAL enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts. This patient has a UNILATERAL solid enhancing mass. PKD does not cause erythrocytosis, hypercalcemia, or renal vein invasion. PKD presents with hypertension, hematuria, flank pain, and progressive CKD.

Reference: RCC - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470336/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with sudden-onset severe LEFT flank pain radiating to his groin. The pain is colicky (comes in waves), and he is writhing and unable to find a comfortable position. He has nausea and one episode of vomiting. He has microscopic hematuria.Vital SignsBP 162/98HR 110LabsCr 1.0Urinalysis RBC >50/hpf, no WBC, no bacteriaCTFinding 6mm stone at left ureterovesical junction with proximal hydroureter What is the management based on stone size and location?

choices.text: All kidney stones require surgical intervention
Ureteral stone (renal colic) — management by SIZE: (1) ≤5mm: 90% pass spontaneously;
NSAIDs are contraindicated for renal colic
Stone location does not affect passage likelihood

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT from appendicitis: Both cause acute abdominal pain with nausea, but:
• COLICKY (waxing/waning) vs STEADY pain
• Radiates to GROIN (follows ureter) vs RLQ localized
• Patient WRITHING (can't find comfortable position) vs STILL (movement worsens peritonitis)
• HEMATURIA (90%+ of stones) vs no hematuria
• NO peritoneal signs vs guarding/rebound

Stone passage by size:
• ≤5mm: 90% pass
• 5-10mm: 50% pass
• >10mm: unlikely to pass

Medical expulsive therapy: tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) relaxes ureteral smooth muscle, increases passage rate. NSAIDs: FIRST-LINE for renal colic (more effective than opioids, reduce ureteral spasm).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Colicky flank pain radiating to groin = classic renal colic
- Writhing, can't get comfortable = visceral pain
- Hematuria confirms urologic source
- 6mm at UVJ = borderline (50% may pass)
- No fever, no pyuria = not infected
- Trial of passage with tamsulosin, NSAIDs, hydration
- Follow-up imaging to confirm passage or plan intervention

Evidence: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - Brisbane W, et al. StatPearls. NBK430845. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- All stones need surgery: Most small stones (≤5mm) pass spontaneously with conservative management. Surgery is reserved for large stones (>10mm), obstructing stones with AKI, infected stones, or intractable symptoms.
- Empiric antibiotics: Antibiotics are only indicated for INFECTED stones (pyuria, fever, sepsis). This patient has hematuria without WBCs or bacteria — the stone is obstructing but not infected. Treating a sterile stone with antibiotics is unnecessary.
- NSAIDs contraindicated: NSAIDs are FIRST-LINE for renal colic — more effective than opioids in RCTs. They reduce ureteral spasm and inflammation. Only contraindicated if AKI or CKD. This patient has normal Cr.
- Location doesn't matter: Location significantly affects passage. Stones at the UVJ (ureterovesical junction) are at the narrowest point but also closest to the bladder. Proximal ureteral stones have longer to travel. UVJ stones often pass but may need intervention if persistent.

Reference: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430845/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 16-year-old boy presents with sudden onset severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 4 hours ago. He had one episode of vomiting. The left testicle is swollen, tender, and riding high with a horizontal lie. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left.Vital SignsTemp 37.2°CHR 98ExamLeft_testicle High-riding, horizontal lie, exquisitely tender, no relief with elevationCremasteric_reflex Absent left, present rightRight_testicle Normal position and reflex What is the diagnosis and the time-sensitive management?

choices.text: TESTICULAR TORSION — twisting of spermatic cord compromises blood supply;
Doppler ultrasound must be obtained before surgery
Attempt manual detorsion and discharge if successful
Torsion of appendix testis — supportive care only

explanation:
Correct Answer: Testicular torsion vs epididymitis:

| Feature | Torsion | Epididymitis |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Onset | Sudden, severe | Gradual |
| Age | Adolescent peak | Sexually active or elderly |
| Cremasteric reflex | ABSENT | Present |
| Testis position | High-riding, horizontal | Normal |
| Prehn's sign | Negative (no relief) | Positive (relief with elevation) |
| Doppler | Decreased/absent flow | Increased flow |

Time = testicle:
• 24 hours: If clinical picture classic: go directly to OR — do NOT delay for imaging. Doppler US only if diagnosis uncertain.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 16-year-old = peak age for torsion
- Sudden onset woke from sleep = classic history
- High-riding + horizontal lie = bell-clapper deformity
- Absent cremasteric reflex = highly sensitive (99%)
- 4 hours since onset = within salvage window
- No relief with elevation = not epididymitis
- EMERGENCY OR: do not delay for imaging
- Bilateral orchiopexy: fix both sides (contralateral also at risk)

Evidence: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - Sharp VJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK448199. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Epididymitis: Epididymitis has GRADUAL onset, PRESENT cremasteric reflex, relief with elevation (positive Prehn's sign), and often dysuria/pyuria (if infectious). It affects sexually active young men (STI: chlamydia, gonorrhea) or older men (UTI: E. coli). This patient has sudden onset, absent reflex, and high-riding testis = TORSION.
- Doppler US before surgery: If clinical picture is CLASSIC for torsion (sudden onset, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding + horizontal testis), do NOT delay surgery for imaging. Time is critical — every hour of delay decreases salvage rate. Doppler is for UNCERTAIN cases only.
- Manual detorsion and discharge: Manual detorsion may be attempted as a temporizing measure (rotate laterally/'open the book'), but even if successful, SURGERY is still required for bilateral orchiopexy. The underlying bell-clapper deformity persists and torsion will recur. Never discharge without surgical fixation.
- Appendix testis torsion: Torsion of the appendix testis (embryonic remnant) causes the 'blue dot sign' (visible through scrotal skin) and is treated conservatively with NSAIDs. However, this patient has high-riding testis, horizontal lie, and absent cremasteric reflex — findings that indicate TRUE testicular torsion, not appendage torsion. These must be distinguished because true torsion is an emergency.

Reference: Testicular Torsion - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448199/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with 2 years of progressive urinary symptoms: weak stream, hesitancy, nocturia (3-4 times/night), incomplete emptying, and post-void dribbling. He denies hematuria or dysuria. PSA is 2.8 ng/mL (normal for age). DRE reveals a uniformly enlarged, smooth, non-tender prostate.LabsPSA 2.8 ng/mLUrinalysis NormalPost-void residual 85 mL (elevated, normal What is the first-line medical treatment and mechanism?

choices.text: Immediate TURP is required for all symptomatic BPH
Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — treatment starts with MEDICAL therapy:
Finasteride alone provides rapid symptom relief
Observation only — BPH is benign and doesn't progress

explanation:
Correct Answer: BPH medical therapy:

| Class | Examples | Mechanism | Onset | Notes |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------|-------|
| Alpha-blockers | Tamsulosin, alfuzosin | Relax smooth muscle | Days-weeks | First-line, any size |
| 5-ARI | Finasteride, dutasteride | Block DHT, shrink prostate | 3-6 months | For large prostate |
| PDE5i | Tadalafil | Smooth muscle relaxation | Days | Also treats ED |

IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score): quantifies symptom severity (0-7 mild, 8-19 moderate, 20-35 severe).

Surgical indications: refractory symptoms, urinary retention, recurrent UTI, bladder stones, renal insufficiency, large post-void residual with recurrent retention.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Progressive LUTS over 2 years = classic BPH history
- Weak stream, hesitancy, nocturia, incomplete emptying = obstructive + irritative symptoms
- Uniformly enlarged smooth prostate = BPH (nodular/hard = cancer concern)
- PSA 2.8 = normal for age (BPH can mildly elevate PSA)
- Normal UA, no hematuria = no red flags for malignancy
- First-line: alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) for symptom relief
- Can add 5-ARI if prostate is large
- Surgery only if medical therapy fails or complications develop

Evidence: BPH - StatPearls - Foster HE, et al. StatPearls. NBK558930. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Immediate TURP: Surgery (TURP) is reserved for FAILED medical therapy or complications (retention, recurrent UTI, bladder stones, renal insufficiency). First-line treatment is medical — alpha-blockers for all, add 5-ARI for large prostate. Most men improve with medications and never need surgery.
- Finasteride for rapid relief: Finasteride (5-ARI) works by shrinking the prostate — this takes 3-6 MONTHS for noticeable effect. It is NOT for rapid symptom relief. Alpha-blockers work within days-weeks by relaxing smooth muscle. For rapid relief, use alpha-blockers; add finasteride for long-term benefit in large prostates.
- Antibiotics for prostatitis: This presentation is classic BPH, not prostatitis. Chronic prostatitis causes perineal/pelvic pain, pain with ejaculation, and often has recurrent UTIs or prostate tenderness. This patient has obstructive voiding symptoms without pain — BPH, not prostatitis. Antibiotics are not indicated.
- Observation only: While watchful waiting is appropriate for MILD symptoms (IPSS <8), this patient has significant symptoms affecting quality of life (nocturia 3-4x, incomplete emptying) and elevated post-void residual. BPH DOES progress and can lead to retention, UTI, and renal damage. Medical therapy is indicated.

Reference: BPH - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK558930/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with 2 weeks of painless gross hematuria. He also reports a dull left flank ache and a 10-lb weight loss. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history. On exam, he has a palpable left flank mass and a new left-sided varicocele.LabsHgb 17.8 (elevated — polycythemia)Ca 11.2 (elevated)Cr 1.1 What findings suggest the diagnosis, and what is the classic triad?

choices.text: Bladder cancer — cystoscopy is the first study needed
Renal cell carcinoma — classic triad: hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass;
Polycystic kidney disease with hemorrhage into a cyst
Renal angiomyolipoma — benign tumor causing hematuria
Adrenal mass with secondary renal compression

explanation:
Correct Answer: RCC classic triad (present in only 10%): hematuria + flank pain + palpable mass. Paraneoplastic syndromes are common (40%): polycythemia (EPO production), hypercalcemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without metastases). LEFT varicocele that doesn't decompress when supine = LEFT renal vein invasion (gonadal vein drains into left renal vein; right drains directly into IVC). Workup: CT abdomen with and without contrast (best initial imaging), CT chest for staging, biopsy NOT needed before surgery (risk of seeding). Treatment: partial nephrectomy (7 cm or locally advanced). RCC is resistant to chemo/radiation. Immunotherapy: nivolumab/ipilimumab for metastatic (checkpoint inhibitors). TKIs: sunitinib, pazopanib.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 58-year-old male smoker = RCC risk factors
- Painless gross hematuria = classic presenting symptom
- Flank mass = large renal tumor
- Weight loss = malignancy red flag
- Left varicocele = left renal vein invasion (pathognomonic)
- Polycythemia (Hgb 17.8) = EPO paraneoplastic production
- Hypercalcemia = PTHrP paraneoplastic
- CT abdomen with contrast = initial imaging

Evidence: Renal Cell Carcinoma - StatPearls - Hsieh JJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK470336. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Bladder cancer: Bladder cancer causes painless hematuria but does NOT cause a palpable flank mass, left varicocele, polycythemia, or hypercalcemia. Cystoscopy is needed to evaluate the bladder, but the flank mass and paraneoplastic features point to a renal primary.
- Polycystic kidney disease: PKD causes bilateral enlarged kidneys with cysts and can bleed, but does NOT cause polycythemia, hypercalcemia, or a new varicocele. PKD patients typically have a family history, bilateral involvement, and associated liver cysts.
- Angiomyolipoma: AML is a benign renal tumor that can cause hemorrhage but is characteristically seen in tuberous sclerosis, contains fat (visible on CT), and does NOT cause paraneoplastic syndromes, varicocele, or weight loss.
- Adrenal mass: Adrenal masses present with hormonal syndromes (Cushing, pheo, Conn) and are typically above/medial to the kidney. A left varicocele specifically indicates LEFT RENAL VEIN compression/invasion, pointing to a renal origin.

Reference: RCC - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470336/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 24-year-old man with sickle cell disease presents with a painful, persistent erection lasting 5 hours. He denies sexual stimulation or medication use. The corpora cavernosa are rigid and tender, while the glans and corpus spongiosum are soft.Vital SignsHR 92BP 138/82 What type of priapism is this, and what determines the urgency?

choices.text: High-flow (non-ischemic) priapism — observation with ice packs is sufficient
Low-flow (ischemic/venoocclusive) priapism — urologic emergency requiring corporal aspiration and phenylephrine
Stuttering priapism — no acute intervention needed, schedule outpatient urology
Pharmacologic priapism from PDE5 inhibitor — terbutaline injection
Priapism resolves spontaneously in sickle cell patients within 12 hours

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ischemic (low-flow) priapism: venoocclusive, PAINFUL, >4 hours = UROLOGIC EMERGENCY. Corporal blood gas: dark blood, pO2 60, pH 4-6 hours → risk of corporal fibrosis → permanent erectile dysfunction. >24 hours → near-certain impotence.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sickle cell disease = most common cause in young adults
- 5-hour painful erection = ischemic priapism
- Rigid corpora cavernosa + soft glans = low-flow pattern
- 5 hours = beyond 4-hour threshold — urgency
- Corporal aspiration + phenylephrine injection = first-line
- Risk of permanent ED increases with each hour of delay

Evidence: Priapism - StatPearls - Broderick GA, et al. StatPearls. NBK459193. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- High-flow — observation: High-flow (non-ischemic) priapism is painless and typically post-traumatic. This patient has a PAINFUL erection from sickle cell disease with no history of trauma — this is ischemic (low-flow), which requires emergent treatment.
- Stuttering priapism: Stuttering priapism refers to recurrent brief episodes (<3 hours) that resolve spontaneously. This patient has had a 5-hour sustained episode, which has crossed the threshold into ischemic emergency territory requiring intervention.
- Terbutaline: Terbutaline (beta-agonist) has been used historically but is less effective than phenylephrine (alpha-agonist) and has more cardiovascular side effects. Phenylephrine is the recommended first-line sympathomimetic.
- Resolves spontaneously in SCD: Priapism in sickle cell does NOT reliably resolve on its own after 4 hours. Waiting increases ischemic damage with each passing hour. Standard treatment (aspiration + phenylephrine) is required, plus sickle cell-specific measures (hydration, exchange transfusion).

Reference: Priapism - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459193/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a painless, firm right testicular mass that he noticed 2 weeks ago. Ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular mass. Tumor markers: AFP 85 ng/mL (elevated), β-hCG 12 mIU/mL (mildly elevated), LDH 280 U/L (elevated). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Observation with repeat ultrasound in 3 months

explanation:
Correct Answer: Painless testicular mass in a young man = testicular cancer until proven otherwise. Radical inguinal orchiectomy is both diagnostic AND therapeutic (NEVER transscrotal biopsy — risk of scrotal seeding and altered lymphatic drainage). AFP elevation suggests nonseminoma (yolk sac tumor component). Staging CT after orchiectomy.

Evidence: Testicular Cancer - StatPearls - Baird DC, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK563159. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Scrotal biopsy: NEVER perform transscrotal biopsy or approach. Risk of scrotal skin seeding and inguinal → pelvic nodal drainage disruption.
- CT first: Staging imaging is done AFTER orchiectomy. Should not delay definitive surgery.
- Neoadjuvant chemo: Upfront chemotherapy is not standard for localized testicular cancer. Orchiectomy first.
- Observation: Solid intratesticular mass with elevated markers is cancer until proven otherwise. Cannot observe.

Reference: Testicular Cancer - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563159/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 42-year-old man presents with acute-onset severe left flank pain radiating to the groin, nausea, and hematuria. CT without contrast shows a 4mm stone at the left ureterovesical junction. Creatinine is normal. No fever. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.text: Medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin and pain control

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ureteral stones 10mm.

Evidence: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - Fontenelle LF, Sarti TD. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK448069. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Emergent surgery: Not indicated for small, uncomplicated stones. Surgery reserved for stones >10mm, infection, or failed conservative management.
- IV antibiotics: No fever or signs of infection. Antibiotics only if infected stone (pyonephrosis) — which is a urologic emergency.
- ESWL: ESWL is effective for proximal stones <2cm but 4mm UVJ stones pass spontaneously 90% of the time with MET.
- Observation without medication: Tamsulosin improves passage rates and reduces time to passage. Pain control is essential for patient comfort.

Reference: Nephrolithiasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448069/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive urinary hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia (3x/night), and incomplete emptying over the past year. AUA Symptom Score is 18 (moderate-severe). DRE shows a smooth, symmetrically enlarged prostate. PSA is 2.8. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
Correct Answer: BPH medical therapy: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, silodosin) are first-line for symptomatic relief — faster onset (days-weeks), improve flow. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) for large prostates (>30-40g) — reduce prostate size over months, prevent progression. Combination therapy for large prostates with moderate-severe symptoms. Tamsulosin is preferred over non-selective doxazosin (fewer cardiovascular side effects).

Evidence: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia - StatPearls - Ng M, Baradhi KM. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK558920. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Finasteride alone: 5ARI takes 6-12 months for effect. Better for large prostates. Not ideal as monotherapy for symptomatic relief.
- Oxybutynin: Anticholinergic for OAB/storage symptoms. Can worsen retention in BPH by reducing detrusor contractility.
- Doxazosin: Non-selective alpha-blocker with significant orthostatic hypotension risk. Tamsulosin (uroselective) preferred.
- TURP: Reserved for refractory symptoms, urinary retention, recurrent UTI, bladder stones, or renal insufficiency from BPH.

Reference: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK558920/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: 68yo man, PSA 8.2 (was 4.1 last year), firm nodule on DRE. MRI PI-RADS 4.Vital SignsBP 138/82HR 74RR 14Temp 98.4FSpO₂ 99% Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Rising PSA, firm DRE nodule, PI-RADS 4 = highly suspicious for prostate adenocarcinoma. MRI-targeted biopsy next.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- PSA velocity >0.75/year
- DRE nodule suggests malignancy
- PI-RADS 4-5: high probability significant cancer
- MRI-targeted biopsy improves detection

- Prostatitis: Tender, boggy, UTI symptoms.
- Abscess: Fever, perineal pain, fluctuant.
- Stricture: Obstructive symptoms, no PSA/DRE abnormality.

explanation:

explanation:

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Left 90% due to venous anatomy
- Leading correctable infertility cause
- New right-sided in adult: rule out renal mass
- Increases with standing, decompresses supine

- Hydrocele: Transilluminates, smooth, non-compressible.
- Epididymal cyst: Discrete mass, doesn't change with position.

explanation:
Correct Answer: Non-contrast CT is gold standard for renal colic. Sensitivity >95% for all stone types. Also identifies size, location, and alternative diagnoses.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 95-98% sensitivity for urolithiasis
- Identifies stone size (10mm intervention)
- Detects alternative diagnoses
- US first-line in pregnancy/peds

- KUB: Misses radiolucent stones; ~60% sensitivity.

- IVP: Replaced by CT.
